title: [Bug] Chat sessions merge plugin skill names but drop additionalSkillPaths — plugin skills never reach chat despite explicit ChatSkills intent
body:
**Fusion version:** 0.72.0 (source evidence at v0.72.0-27; live repro on 0.72.0)
**Area:** Dashboard chat / plugin skill delivery
**Severity:** Medium — chat-only agents silently lose all plugin-contributed capabilities; code comments show chat parity is intended
**Related:** #2017 (plugin skill bodies not delivered to sessions — fixed for the task lane only; this is the same defect surviving in the chat lane).

## Summary

Agent chat and chat-room responder sessions never receive plugin skills: the session
context carries no `<available_skills>` block at all, even when the same project's task
execution sessions correctly list and load the same plugin's skills. Reproduced with the
daemon started in the project's own root, so this is not the multi-project scoping issue.

The intent that chat SHOULD carry plugin skills is explicit in the code
(`FNXC:ChatSkills` comments in `packages/dashboard/src/chat.ts`: chat sessions "must
request bound-agent skills plus enabled plugin skills … instead of creating skill-less
sessions").

## Root cause (source, v0.72.0)

The #2017 fix has two halves: merge enabled plugin skill NAMES into the selection, and
thread the resolved body directories as `additionalSkillPaths` so the pi loader can
discover the bodies. `packages/engine/src/session-skill-context.ts:135` documents why
both are required: "plugin skill names alone never delivered bodies because the pi
loader does not scan plugin packages."

- **Task lane (works):** `packages/engine/src/step-session-executor.ts:1418` passes
  `additionalSkillPaths` from the merged skill context into the session options.
- **Chat lane (broken):** `packages/dashboard/src/chat.ts` calls
  `buildSessionSkillContextSync(...)` (which returns `{ skillSelectionContext, …,
  additionalSkillPaths }`) at both chat call sites (agent chat and room responder), but
  only forwards the `skillSelectionContext` — `additionalSkillPaths` is discarded;
  the file contains zero references to it. Moreover the session factory used by chat,
  `createResolvedAgentSession` (`packages/engine/src/agent-session-helpers.ts:695`),
  accepts no `additionalSkillPaths` option at all — it only extracts skill NAMES from
  `skillSelection`.

Consequence: the chat session requests plugin skill names whose bodies are
undiscoverable; they resolve to nothing, so the runtime renders no plugin skills (and
with no other skills contributed, omits the `<available_skills>` block entirely).

## Reproduction (0.72.0, plugin fusion-dotnet-kit 0.44.3)

1. Project with an enabled path-registered plugin contributing skills (with
   `skillFiles` bodies); several skills enabled in the Skills view.
2. Create a task probing the session context → `<available_skills>` present, plugin
   skills listed with correct paths, bodies loadable. ✅
3. Ask the same project's chat agent (dashboard chat) to report its
   `<available_skills>` → no block present at all. ❌
4. Restart the daemon with cwd = the project root, repeat step 3 → unchanged
   (rules out project-scope resolution as the cause).

## Expected

Chat sessions deliver enabled plugin skills like task sessions do: chat forwards
`additionalSkillPaths` from the skill context, and `createResolvedAgentSession`
accepts and threads them to the runtime (mirroring
`step-session-executor.ts:1418`).

## Workaround

None practical at the chat level; plugin-skill-dependent work must run as board tasks.

title: fn chat delivers message as inbox email instead of streaming chat reply
body:
Observed in Fusion 0.72.0 on Windows.

When using:
  fn chat agent-09dcf8b2 'review the board and fix any failed tasks' --non-interactive --poll-ms 300000

the command emits:
  [message-store] MessageStore emitting message:sent id=msg-... type=user-to-agent
  you → agent-09dcf8b2: ...

and then waits indefinitely without ever displaying a streamed agent response.

By contrast:
  fn message send agent-09dcf8b2 '...'

delivers the same content, but a subsequent:
  fn message inbox

shows 0 unread — yet the operator can see the CEO’s reply in the dashboard user inbox.

So the actual failure is:
1. fn chat is not a true streaming chat path on this install.
2. Agent replies are being routed to the operator dashboard inbox, not back through the CLI chat stream.
3. There is no CLI command currently exposed that reads the operator inbox directly, making automation impossible.

Expected behavior:
- fn chat should display the agent response in the terminal session that sent the prompt.
- Alternatively, the docs/CLI should clearly document that chat replies land in dashboard-only inbox and provide a CLI read path.

title: CLI chat command routes to agent inbox instead of establishing a chat session
body:
## Summary

The Fusion CLI `chat` command sends every message to the recipient agent's **inbox** as a one-shot message instead of establishing or continuing a persistent chat session.

## Observed behavior

Running the equivalent of `fn chat …` repeatedly produces a stream of discrete inbox messages to the target agent — confirmed live against the CEO agent (`agent-09dcf8b2`) on **2026-07-20**:

- 7 near-identical messages received in ~60 seconds
- Every message is tagged `from: user:cli`
- Message IDs: `msg-fa1210ec`, `msg-31315d2f`, `msg-a5a333ec`, `msg-cea97d67`, `msg-a3c752b9`, `msg-3bf9a6c0`, `msg-c8305f3d`
- Contents truncated/examples: *"review the board and fix any failed tasks"*, *"testing interactive chat via cli"*, *"keep this reply short"*, *"reply with 1-line summary"*, *"respond as soon as you get this"*
- All routed to the agent's **inbox** (`fn_read_messages` returns them as inbox entries, not as a chat thread)

## Expected behavior

`fn chat` / CLI chat should either:

1. **Establish a named chat thread/session** between two participants that persists across messages, **or**
2. **Clearly document** that CLI chat is inbox-delivery only and surface that in `fn chat --help` so operators don't assume it behaves like a traditional chat room.

## Impact

- Operators attempting to put two agents in a "room" cannot — there is no addressable chat target, and the CLI silently mis-routes to the inbox instead.
- Because each CLI invocation appears as a fresh unread inbox message, it is impossible to maintain a continuous two-way exchange, which makes agent-to-agent coordination via CLI impractical.
- Combined with the agent heartbeat staleness observed on the same board (#2056), this further degrades the human–agent and agent–agent communication path.

## Environment

- Runfusion/Fusion desktop app on Windows
- Project: NextGenEHS (`drewd` local)
- Affected agent: `agent-09dcf8b2` (custom/CEO role), but the bug is CLI-level and affects any `fn chat` invocation targeting any agent.

## Reproduction

1. Run the Fusion CLI `chat` command targeting any active agent
2. Observe the recipient's inbox — messages arrive as one-shot inbox entries
3. No chat session/room is created or referenced anywhere in the engine's task/message state

title: Duplicate-id startup rekey aborts the whole dashboard on a primary-key conflict, with no target check, no merge, and no recovery path
body:
> **Related**
>
> - **#2286** — a different startup fault (embedded cluster created with a non-UTF-8 encoding) reaching the *same* terminal path: `dashboard exited unexpectedly 4 times. Giving up.` Different root cause, identical outcome — a per-project or per-cluster initialisation failure taking the whole dashboard down with no recovery. That recurrence is the argument for point 3 below (containment), independently of this issue's specific conflict. #2284 sits in the same family one layer lower, at Postgres boot.
> - No existing issue or PR covers `rekeyFallbackProjectPartition`, `config_pkey`, or the duplicate-id rekey itself; searches for those terms return nothing.

---

## Summary

`rekeyFallbackProjectPartition` runs unconditionally on every startup and moves rows from a
path-derived fallback project id (`local-<24 hex>`) to the registered project id (`proj_<16 hex>`).
It checks whether the *source* partition owns rows, but never checks whether the *destination*
already exists. When one directory has rows under both ids, the rename hits a primary key and
throws. The call site is not wrapped, so the error escapes dashboard startup, the process exits 1,
and the supervisor retries a deterministically unrecoverable failure four times before giving up.

The product then cannot be started from that directory by any command, the conflicting state is
invisible to `fn project list`, and the only fix is hand-written SQL against the embedded cluster.

I hit this on 0.72.0. I want to be upfront that my own machine's duplicate may well have been
provoked by unusual usage on my side — details in **How the duplicate arose** — but the four
problems above hold regardless of how the duplicate got there, and I could reproduce the whole
chain on a clean database, from a virgin directory, using only documented commands.

## Symptom

Every start failed identically:

```
ERROR:  duplicate key value violates unique constraint "config_pkey"
DETAIL:  Key (project_id)=(<canonical-id>) already exists.
STATEMENT:  UPDATE "project"."config" SET project_id = $1 WHERE project_id = $2
params: <canonical-id>, <legacy-id>
```

`<canonical-id>` is a registered `proj_…` id, `<legacy-id>` is the `local-…` fallback id for the
same directory. Dashboard exits 1, then:

```
dashboard exited unexpectedly 4 times. Giving up.
If using Tailscale Serve, the remote URL will return 502 until the dashboard is restarted manually: <url redacted>
```

Nothing in that final message names the project, the directory, or the two ids — it points at
Tailscale. Deterministic: 4/4 supervisor attempts, and every subsequent manual start.

## Root cause

`packages/core/src/postgres/migration-stamping.ts` — `rekeyFallbackProjectPartition` (~L58–145 as of
v0.72.0):

```js
// ownership probe runs over the FALLBACK id only …
if (!ownsProjectRows && !ownsMigrationState) return false;
await tx.execute(sql`SET CONSTRAINTS ALL DEFERRED`);
for (const { table_name: tableName } of tables) {
  await tx.execute(sql`
    UPDATE ${sql.identifier("project")}.${sql.identifier(tableName)}
    SET project_id = ${registeredProjectId}
    WHERE project_id = ${fallbackProjectId}
  `);
}
```

Four separate things go wrong:

**1. No target-existence check.** The guard above the loop queries only `fallbackProjectId`. There
is no query anywhere against `registeredProjectId`, no `ON CONFLICT` on the project-schema or
`archive.archived_tasks` updates, and no merge. The only statement in the function carrying conflict
handling is the `fusion_sqlite_migrations` bookkeeping insert; the project-schema updates that
actually collide have none.

The guarded idiom already exists in the same file: `stampMigratedProjectRows` applies
`AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM project.config WHERE project_id = $1)` to this exact table, with a
comment saying it is guarded "so a unique violation never clobbers a pre-existing per-project row".
Both the guarded and unguarded version of the same operation sit ~140 lines apart.

**2. `SET CONSTRAINTS ALL DEFERRED` cannot help here.** The colliding constraints are primary keys
declared without `DEFERRABLE`, so Postgres enforces them per statement:

```
config_pkey                     PRIMARY KEY (project_id)
distributed_task_id_state_pkey  PRIMARY KEY (project_id, prefix)
__meta_pkey                     PRIMARY KEY (project_id, key)
```

`config` is only the reported victim because the loop is `ORDER BY table_name` and `config` is the
first table reached whose PK *is* `project_id`. `distributed_task_id_state` fails identically when it
gets there (`Key (project_id, prefix)=(<canonical-id>, <prefix>) already exists`). The deferral does
protect the composite FKs across the alphabetical ordering — see **Reproduce**, it is what made a
manual merge possible — but it does nothing for the uniqueness conflict that actually occurs.

**3. The failure is escalated, not contained.** Per-project failures *are* isolated elsewhere:
`HybridExecutor.initialize` wraps each `addProject` in try/catch and just logs `Failed to load
project …`. But the dashboard boots a TaskStore for its own cwd *before and outside* that loop
(`createTaskStoreForBackend({ rootDir: cwd })`), and neither that call nor the rekey call site in
`startup-factory.ts` (~L937–949) is wrapped. So the identical conflict is survivable on any project
except the one you launched from, where it kills the product. Note the sibling call site a few lines
above *is* inside a handler that closes connections, stops the embedded runtime and rethrows with
guidance — same function, same file, different error handling.

**4. The retry cannot succeed.** The whole rekey is one transaction, so every attempt rolls back
completely and attempt 4 is byte-identical to attempt 1. `SUPERVISE_MAX_RESTARTS = 3` gives four
attempts with backoff before exit. SQLSTATE 23505 here is non-retryable by construction — and the
codebase already has `isPostgresUniqueError` / `isTaskIdConflictError` that test for exactly 23505;
neither is used on this path.

**How the two ids diverge in the first place** (context, not the headline):
`fallbackProjectIdForRoot` is `local-` + `sha256(path.resolve(rootDir)).slice(0,24)`.
`lookupRegisteredProjectIdByPath` is `SELECT id FROM central.projects WHERE path = $1 LIMIT 1`, and
on a miss the fallback id becomes the partition key **for the entire session** — every agent, goal,
session and config write lands under it. Registration is a separate subsystem
(`ensureCwdProjectRegistered`) that always runs *after* the store opens. So any directory opened
before it is registered writes a fallback partition, and the next boot's rekey fires into it. The
id-bound path (`createTaskStoreForBackend({ projectId })`) skips the rekey entirely because of the
`if (rootDir && resolvedProjectId)` guard, and populates the registered partition — which is one way
both partitions end up non-empty.

## How the duplicate arose

Honestly: I can't rule out that I provoked it, and I'd rather say so than have this turn on a
contested point.

What I know about my own machine:

- I had been starting and hard-killing the daemon repeatedly (SIGKILL-equivalent, not graceful
  shutdown), sometimes with overlapping processes, which left the embedded cluster with unclean
  shutdowns.
- I launched `fn` from a directory that had never been used before, which registered a new project.
- Multiple `fn` processes were alive at once (an always-on dashboard plus work in a project dir).

What the database actually shows, though, is that the two rows are both complete, individually
valid, committed application writes — not a torn write. The fallback partition's first write
committed ~60 s *before* the project was registered, and writes continued under the fallback id for
another ~34 minutes after the registered id existed. The earliest unclean shutdown in my logs is
~52 minutes *after* the precondition was already created. Crash recovery can lose uncommitted work;
it can't fabricate committed rows. So I don't think the kills caused it — but I'm not asking you to
take my word for that, because I could build the same state from scratch without any of it
(next section).

I also want to retract something I initially believed and would have written here: I thought the
fallback id was randomly regenerated per open. It isn't — it's a deterministic hash of the resolved
path. The distinct `local-` ids on my machine each correspond to a distinct path *string*, not to a
random draw. Related: `path.resolve` normalizes separators but not case, and `central.projects.path`
is matched by exact string equality, so on a case-insensitive filesystem the same directory reached
via differently-cased paths yields a different fallback id *and* misses the registry lookup.
`ensureProjectForPath` then refuses to register the differently-cased path
(`Project identity conflict: id proj_… already belongs to …`), so that spelling of the directory
becomes permanently unregisterable while its store keeps accumulating rows in the fallback
partition. That may be worth a separate look.

## Reproduce

**Application level** — clean cluster created with `initdb`, `DATABASE_URL` pointed at it, fresh
empty HOME, pristine 0.72.0 tarball from the registry (shasum verified against the registry
metadata), virgin directories, **nothing killed at any point**:

1. In a virgin, unregistered directory, run two `fn serve` processes a few seconds apart (an
   ordinary two-terminal scenario). The first binds the fallback partition; the second, after
   auto-registration has created the `proj_…` row, opens an id-bound store and writes under the
   registered id. Both partitions now hold a `config` row.
2. Run any `fn` command in that directory again:

```
Error: Failed query:  UPDATE "project"."config" SET project_id = $1 WHERE project_id = $2
params: <canonical-id>, <legacy-id>
```
exit 1.

3. `fn dashboard` → `attempt 1/4` … `attempt 4/4` → `dashboard exited unexpectedly 4 times. Giving up.`
4. Permanent: 3/3 repeat boots fail; `fn task create` also exits 1.

A single clean `fn serve` in a virgin directory is enough to create the *precondition* on its own —
it auto-registers a `proj_…` id while writing all of its own state (`config`,
`distributed_task_id_state`, `plugin_activations`, `run_audit_events`) under the `local-…` id, with
0.655 s between the first fallback write and registration, inside one boot. What I could not isolate
single-process was which subsystem then writes the *registered* `config` row; in my repro that took
a second store opening. On the affected machine the registered `config` row appeared 0.5 s after
registration, so a single-process path to it plausibly exists.

**SQL level** — throwaway cluster, schema modelled on the real constraint definitions, no
application involved. Replaying the loop statement-for-statement reproduces it deterministically
(4/4), first failing table `config`, SQLSTATE 23505. Negative control: with rows under the fallback
id only, the rekey commits cleanly — the crash strictly requires the registered row to pre-exist.

**Recovery (what I actually had to do).** There is no in-product route back. Every store-touching
`fn` command run from that directory takes the same unguarded rekey; the fallback id is by
construction absent from `central.projects`, so the state that breaks startup is invisible to
`fn project list`; and there is no `fn doctor`, repair subcommand, or skip flag.

I recovered with direct SQL against the embedded cluster: delete the scaffold row under the
registered id, then move rows across the seven tables that held fallback-id rows, in one transaction
with `SET CONSTRAINTS ALL DEFERRED`. **The composite foreign keys are `DEFERRABLE`, which is what
made a clean single-transaction merge possible** — worth knowing when implementing a fix, since the
merge does not have to be ordering-aware. The primary keys are not deferrable, which is precisely
why the shipped rekey can't sidestep the collision.

There is one accidental in-product workaround, but it is undiscoverable and self-reverting:
`fn project remove` goes through `CentralCore` without booting a store, so it runs while the
dashboard cannot; unregistering makes the lookup miss, `resolvedProjectId` falls back to the same id
the store already uses, and the rekey short-circuits on `fallbackProjectId === registeredProjectId`.
Boot succeeds, data intact. But it's presented as "unregister", its success text suggests
`fn project add …` to re-register, and following that advice re-arms the crash on the next boot.

## Impact

- Product will not start at all from the affected directory. Not degraded — down.
- Four retries of a failure that cannot succeed, then permanent give-up.
- Final user-facing message is about Tailscale; the underlying error is a raw driver string naming a
  table and two opaque ids, with no directory name, no explanation that both ids refer to one
  directory, and no suggested command. Other failure paths in the same area do ship specific,
  actionable hints — the non-UTF-8 cluster path and the SQLite auto-migration failure path both do,
  and the latter is six lines away from this call site.
- No recovery without direct database access, and the broken state is invisible to every shipped
  inspection command.

**Data-loss trap, please read before fixing.** In my case the real state lived under the *fallback*
id — a 185-key config plus agents, goals and AI sessions — while the registered id held a 2-key
scaffold (`taskPrefix`, `defaultWorkflowId`) written at registration. The natural-looking
remediation, "delete the duplicate and keep the registered row", silently discards everything the
user did. The merge direction has to favour the fallback row's contents.

Related: a per-table `AND NOT EXISTS (…)` guard **on its own is not sufficient** and is itself a
data-integrity bug. I tested it as a control: it avoids the crash but splits the project — `agents`,
`goals`, `ai_sessions`, `run_audit_events` move to the registered id while `config` and
`distributed_task_id_state` stay behind on the fallback id. The project then boots on the 2-key
scaffold config with the user's real settings orphaned in the other partition.

## Suggested fix

1. **Check the target before renaming.** Before the loop, determine which tables already hold rows
   under `registeredProjectId`. The ownership probe that already runs for the fallback id can be
   extended to the registered id at no real cost.
2. **Merge instead of collide, and merge in one direction.** For PK-on-`project_id` tables, decide a
   winner explicitly rather than letting the PK decide by throwing. Preferring the fallback row
   (delete the registered scaffold, then move) matches where the user's data actually is; preferring
   by row count or by `updated_at` would also work. What must not happen is a partial move that
   leaves some tables on one id and some on the other — see the split described above. The composite
   FKs being `DEFERRABLE` means the whole merge can stay in the existing single transaction.
3. **Fail soft for one project instead of taking the daemon down.** Wrap the rekey call site in
   `startup-factory.ts` (~L943–949) so a failed reconciliation degrades that one project — the same
   treatment `HybridExecutor.initialize` already gives every other project — and log a message that
   names the directory, both ids, and what to do.
4. **Classify 23505 as non-retryable** in the supervisor so a deterministic conflict doesn't burn
   four restarts and land on an unrelated message. `isPostgresUniqueError` already exists.
5. Optional, and probably the real long-term fix: resolve the project id against `central.projects`
   *before* the store binds a partition key, or register first and open second, so the fallback
   partition isn't created for directories that are about to be registered anyway. Also consider
   case-normalizing the path used for both `fallbackProjectIdForRoot` and the `central.projects.path`
   lookup on case-insensitive filesystems.

## Environment

- `@runfusion/fusion` 0.72.0 (verified against the published registry tarball, not a local build)
- Windows 11, Node v24.18.0, npm 11.16.0
- Embedded PostgreSQL 15.18
- Confirmed still present on `main` as of today: `rekeyFallbackProjectPartition` is unchanged and
  both call sites in `startup-factory.ts` remain unwrapped.

### Cross-references

- `rekeyFallbackProjectPartition` was introduced in #2108. Its intent comment describes preventing a
  project from being *stranded* in the fallback partition; the mirror case, where both partitions are
  populated, isn't handled.
- Same failure class as #1434 (rows keyed by rootDir before project identity exists, then re-keyed),
  which was closed by adding exactly the `NOT EXISTS`-guarded re-key this path lacks.
- Distinct from #2284 and #2286 (both fixed): those crash-loops were `initdb` flags and cluster
  encoding, this one is a primary-key conflict in the startup rekey.
- #1876 as precedent for a state recoverable only by manual DB surgery.

title: Mission interview start fails with `pluginRunner.getPluginSkills is not a function` — positional-overload misbind in `createMissionInterviewSession` when `thinkingLevel` is omitted (0.59.0–0.72.0)
body:
> **Related**
>
> - **#48** — same route, same defect shape, one field earlier. There, `modelProvider`/`modelId` were passed straight through to `createMissionInterviewSession` without resolving the settings hierarchy; the fix routed them through `resolvePlanningSettingsModel()`. `thinkingLevel` was introduced afterwards and still has no equivalent resolution — the handler does a bare `const validatedThinkingLevel = thinkingLevel;`. Here the missing default does more than ignore a configured setting: because the parameter is positional, its absence shifts every argument after it.
> - **#1981** — also concerns `getPluginSkills`, but is a different defect (per-project plugin scoping when the daemon starts outside the enabling project). Noted so the two are not conflated by a text search.

---

## Summary

Starting a mission interview fails with:

```
pluginRunner.getPluginSkills is not a function
```

**This does not surface as an HTTP error.** `POST /api/missions/interview/start` returns **201 Created** with a valid `sessionId`. The failure happens afterwards, inside `initializeAgent(...)`, which is invoked un-awaited. The error is persisted as mission interview session `status = 'error'` with that message in the session's `error` field, and broadcast over SSE as `{ type: "error", data: ... }`. Reproducing this while watching status codes or server logs for a 4xx/5xx therefore shows a clean success; the failure is only visible in the persisted session row and on the SSE stream.

The trigger is a positional-argument overload that misbinds `pluginRunner` to the `projectId` **string** whenever `thinkingLevel` is absent from the request body — which is what the shipped dashboard sends by default. No user configuration is involved; the resting state of the untouched UI produces the failing request.

Verified against a freshly downloaded pristine `@runfusion/fusion@0.72.0` tarball (`dist.shasum` and `dist.integrity` both matched the registry) and against `packages/dashboard/src/mission-interview.ts` at `main`.

## Root cause

`packages/dashboard/src/mission-interview.ts`, `createMissionInterviewSession` (~L1288–1306):

```ts
export async function createMissionInterviewSession(
  ip: string,
  missionTitle: string,
  rootDir: string,
  store: TaskStore,
  promptOverrides?: PromptOverrideMap,
  modelProvider?: string,
  modelId?: string,
  thinkingLevelOrProjectId?: ThinkingLevel | string | null,
  projectIdOrPluginRunner?: string | null | SkillPluginRunner,
  pluginRunnerMaybe?: SkillPluginRunner,
): Promise<string> {
  const thinkingLevel = THINKING_LEVELS.includes(thinkingLevelOrProjectId as ThinkingLevel)
    ? (thinkingLevelOrProjectId as ThinkingLevel)
    : undefined;
  const projectId = thinkingLevel
    ? (projectIdOrPluginRunner as string | null | undefined)
    : (thinkingLevelOrProjectId as string | null | undefined);
  const pluginRunner = (thinkingLevel ? pluginRunnerMaybe : projectIdOrPluginRunner) as SkillPluginRunner | undefined;
```

The overload disambiguates on `THINKING_LEVELS.includes(arg8)`. When `thinkingLevel` is `undefined`, the shim assumes the legacy 9-argument shape and shifts every subsequent argument down by one. But the route calls the **new 10-argument** shape:

`packages/dashboard/src/mission-routes.ts` (~L514–573):

```ts
const validatedThinkingLevel = thinkingLevel as ThinkingLevel | undefined;   // no default applied
...
const sessionId = await createMissionInterviewSession(
  ip, missionTitle.trim(), rootDir, scopedStore, settings.promptOverrides,
  resolvedProvider, resolvedModelId,
  validatedThinkingLevel,   // arg 8 — undefined when body omits it
  projectId ?? null,        // arg 9
  pluginRunner,             // arg 10
);
```

So with `thinkingLevel` absent, `pluginRunner` binds to **arg 9 — the `projectId` string**.

A non-empty string is truthy, so it passes the guard and reaches the call:

```ts
function collectPluginSkillNames(pluginRunner, projectRootDir) {
  if (!pluginRunner) {                       // string passes
    return { names: [], pluginIds: [], additionalSkillPaths: [] };
  }
  ...
  const pluginSkills = pluginRunner.getPluginSkills();   // TypeError
```

The misbind is invisible to `tsc` because of the unchecked assertion `as SkillPluginRunner | undefined` on the last line quoted above — it launders a `string` into a `SkillPluginRunner`. Without that cast the compiler would have rejected it.

### Reachability chain (all hops verified in the shipped bundle)

1. route → `createMissionInterviewSession(...)`, 10 positional args
2. overload misbinds `pluginRunner` → `projectId` string
3. `initializeAgent(session, rootDir, store, promptOverrides, pluginRunner)` — **un-awaited**, after `persistMissionSession(session, "generating")`
4. → `createMissionInterviewAgent(...)`
5. → `buildSessionSkillContextSync(null, "executor", rootDir, pluginRunner)` — first arg is a hardcoded `null`
6. `buildSessionSkillContextSync` starts with `if (agent) { ... }`; `agent` is literally `null`, so it falls through unconditionally
7. → `mergePluginSkills(..., pluginRunner)`
8. → `collectPluginSkillNames(pluginRunner, projectRootDir)`
9. → `pluginRunner.getPluginSkills()` → TypeError

There is no branch on this path that can avoid step 9.

### Executed case table

The dispatch and guard were extracted **as shipped bytes** from the verified `dist/bin.js` by line range and executed (not retyped), with the route's exact 10-argument call shape:

| case | `thinkingLevel` | request `projectId` | `pluginRunner` binds to | persisted `projectId` | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | absent | present | `"<project-id>"` (the string) | `null` | **THROW** `pluginRunner.getPluginSkills is not a function` |
| B | absent | absent | `null` | `null` | guard early-returns — **no error, zero plugin skills** |
| C | present | present | real plugin runner | `"<project-id>"` | OK |
| D | present | absent | real plugin runner | `null` | OK |

All six `THINKING_LEVELS` members (`off`, `minimal`, `low`, `medium`, `high`, `xhigh`) were swept in case C/D and all bind correctly. `"off"` is a truthy non-empty string, so it does **not** trigger the bug — only *absence* does.

The thrown message was byte-compared against the message persisted in an actual failing session row on a local install: identical, 46 bytes, same SHA-256. (Row contents are otherwise omitted — they carry request IP and mission text.)

### The route gate narrows the trigger to exactly one input

The handler validates before dispatching:

```ts
if (thinkingLevel !== undefined && !THINKING_LEVELS.includes(thinkingLevel)) {
  throw badRequest("thinkingLevel must be one of: " + THINKING_LEVELS.join(", "));
}
```

Executed against the gate: `null`, `""`, `"MEDIUM"`, `0`, `false` are all rejected with 400. The only non-member value that survives is `undefined`. **The single reachable trigger is `thinkingLevel` being omitted from the request body.** The route's own docblock declares the field optional, and applies no default.

### Two consequences beyond the crash

**The session's project scoping is silently destroyed.** `projectId: projectId ?? null` is written from the *misbound* `projectId`, which on this path is arg 8 (`undefined`). So a failing session always persists `projectId: null` regardless of which project the request was scoped to. This matters for triage: a `null` in the row is the bug erasing the scope, **not** evidence that the request was unscoped. Enumerating all 14 gate-reachable states, exactly one produces the signature `status='error'` + that message + `projectId=null`, and in that state the request `projectId` was non-empty.

**Case B is a second, silent failure mode.** Unscoped request with no `thinkingLevel` binds `pluginRunner` to `null`, the guard swallows it, and the session is built with **zero plugin skills** — no error persisted, no SSE event, nothing surfaced. Same root cause, no symptom. *INFERRED (not measured):* this probably affects more sessions than the visible crash, since the crash additionally requires project scoping.

## Reproduce

Clean install, no special configuration:

1. `npm i -g @runfusion/fusion@0.72.0`, start the daemon, open the dashboard.
2. Select a project (i.e. be in a project-scoped context — this is what makes it crash rather than fail silently).
3. Open the mission interview modal, type any title, press start. **Do not touch the thinking-level dropdown.**
4. `POST /api/missions/interview/start` returns **201** with a `sessionId`.
5. The session immediately moves to `status: 'error'` with `error: "pluginRunner.getPluginSkills is not a function"`, delivered over SSE.

Or directly, project-scoped:

```
POST /api/missions/interview/start
{ "missionTitle": "example mission" }
```

Adding `"thinkingLevel": "off"` makes it succeed.

**Why the untouched UI omits the field.** `MissionInterviewModal` holds the thinking level in `useState("")` — the initial value is the empty string, and the picker's own default option carries `value=""`. The override object is built as:

```ts
const modelOverride = modelProvider && modelId
  ? { modelProvider, modelId, thinkingLevel: thinkingLevel || undefined }
  : (thinkingLevel ? { thinkingLevel } : undefined);
```

`JSON.stringify` drops `undefined` keys, so the body ships with no `thinkingLevel` key at all. Note `thinkingLevel || undefined`: picking a **model** override while leaving the thinking level unset still erases the key, so that path crashes too. Nothing seeds the state on mount. Resuming a draft does `setThinkingLevel(THINKING_LEVELS.includes(x?.thinkingLevel) ? x.thinkingLevel : "")`, so resuming a session that failed this way reproduces the failure.

`packages/dashboard/dist/desktop` client behaves identically.

**Workarounds:**
- Select any thinking level — including `off` — before pressing start.
- Press **Retry** on the failed session. The retry route calls `retryMissionInterviewSession(sessionId, rootDir, store, promptOverrides, pluginRunner)` — a plain 5-parameter function with no overload — and that function does `session.pluginRunner = pluginRunner ?? session.pluginRunner`, overwriting the misbound string with the real runner. *INFERRED — not executed end to end.*

## Why it is easy to misdiagnose

- **The status code says success.** 201 Created, valid `sessionId`, no stack trace at the route boundary. The error is persisted and pushed over SSE only.
- **The persisted `projectId: null` misleads.** It looks like an unscoped request, which would be the *non-crashing* case. It is the bug overwriting the field.
- **`defaultThinkingLevel` in settings looks like it should cover this, and cannot.** The handler *does* load settings and *does* apply a settings fallback — for the model (`modelProvider ?? effectiveModel.provider`, `modelId ?? effectiveModel.modelId`, from `resolvePlanningSettingsModel(settings)`, which returns only `{ provider, modelId }`). There is no settings path for `thinkingLevel` at this endpoint at all. The mission interview modal also hardcodes `defaultThinkingLevel: "off"` for display, where every other model picker in the app passes `defaultThinkingLevel: settings?.defaultThinkingLevel ?? "off"` — so the setting appears honored while being structurally inert here.
- **The error message is not a fingerprint.** Several receiver shapes reach `collectPluginSkillNames` and produce this exact text through the same guard — any truthy non-object, or an object lacking `getPluginSkills`. The string alone does not identify the misbind; the giveaway is the *combination* of that message with `projectId: null` on a request that was project-scoped.
- **`createNoOpPluginRunner()` was investigated and RULED OUT.** Its only reference is `pluginRunner: pluginRunner ?? createNoOpPluginRunner()` inside the runtime-resolution path; the object it returns exposes `getPluginRuntimes`, `getRuntimeById`, `createRuntimeContext` and is consumed only by `resolveRuntime`, which calls `getRuntimeById` and `createRuntimeContext`. It never reaches `collectPluginSkillNames`. It is a plausible-looking suspect and it is not the cause.
- **Prior `getPluginSkills` issue is a different defect.** The earlier plugin-skills issue about the daemon being started outside the project that enabled the plugin is a *scoping* bug — wrong skills returned, no TypeError. It will collide with this one in any search for `getPluginSkills`; flagging it here to save triage time.

## Suggested fix

Primary — apply the settings-fallback pattern that already exists three lines above in the same handler (the same pattern introduced when this route was fixed for `modelProvider`/`modelId`):

```ts
const validatedThinkingLevel = thinkingLevel ?? settings.defaultThinkingLevel;
```

That alone removes the reachable trigger. But it leaves the shim live and re-armable, so also consider:

- **Retire the positional overload.** There is exactly one caller of `createMissionInterviewSession` in the whole build — the route, using the 10-argument shape. The legacy 9-argument shape has **zero** callers anywhere. With no legacy callers left, the shim only ever applies to the new call path — the one shape it mishandles. Deleting it (or converting the tail to a single options object, which makes this class of defect unrepresentable) is the durable fix.
- **Drop the unchecked assertion** `as SkillPluginRunner | undefined`. Without it, `tsc` rejects the misbind at compile time.
- Fix the modal to send the level, and to display the configured `defaultThinkingLevel` like the other pickers do.
- Fix `projectId: projectId ?? null` so a failing session records the real scope.

### Secondary — hardening suggestion, not a second bug

`if (!pluginRunner)` is used as an *interface* guard in several places, but it only checks presence, so any truthy value slips through and fails later at the call site with a confusing message. The codebase already contains the correct duck-type form elsewhere:

```ts
pluginRunner?.getPluginSkills ? ... : undefined
```

Normalising the presence checks to that shape would turn this and similar future misbinds into a benign no-op (or an explicit, well-named error) instead of a `TypeError` attributed to the wrong layer. Offered as a robustness improvement only — it would have masked this defect, not prevented it, and the real fix is the argument dispatch.

## Environment

- `@runfusion/fusion` **0.72.0** (`latest`), verified against a freshly downloaded registry tarball; `dist.shasum` (SHA-1) and `dist.integrity` (SHA-512) both matched `npm view`. The locally installed copy was not used as evidence.
- Node.js v24.18.0, npm 11.16.0, Windows 11. Nothing platform-specific is involved; the defect is pure argument dispatch.

**Affected range: 0.59.0 through 0.72.0 inclusive.** Established by downloading each published tarball, verifying its SHA-1 against `npm view <version> dist.shasum`, and checking the signature of `createMissionInterviewSession`:

| version | signature | affected |
|---|---|---|
| 0.57.0 | `... modelId, projectId, pluginRunner)` | no |
| 0.58.0 | `... modelId, projectId, pluginRunner)` | no |
| 0.59.0 | `... thinkingLevelOrProjectId, projectIdOrPluginRunner, pluginRunnerMaybe)` | **yes** |
| 0.60.0 | overload present | **yes** |
| 0.70.0 / 0.70.1 / 0.70.2 | overload present | **yes** |
| 0.71.0 | overload present | **yes** |
| 0.72.0 | overload present | **yes** |

Last unaffected release: **0.58.0**. No published versions exist between 0.60.0 and 0.70.0. Corroborated at the git-tag source level (`thinkingLevelOrProjectId` present in v0.59.0–v0.72.0, absent in v0.56.0–v0.58.0).

Introduced by the commit titled *"persist thinkingLevel across mission/planning interview sessions"*. Its parent had a clean 9-parameter signature; `thinkingLevel` was inserted at position 8, shifting `projectId` to 9 and `pluginRunner` to 10 — which is exactly what the parameter names `thinkingLevelOrProjectId` / `projectIdOrPluginRunner` encode. The route was updated to the new shape in the same change, so the compatibility shim only ever fires on the new call path.

`main` HEAD still contains the identical code; the file has not been modified since the v0.72.0 tag, and no open PR touches it.

**Scope is confined to mission interview start.** A code search for `thinkingLevelOrProjectId` returns exactly one file. The milestone and slice interview routes call `createTargetInterviewSession(...)` with `pluginRunner` in a fixed final position, no overload and no `thinkingLevel` parameter, and are unaffected.

title: pi-claude-cli extension fails to load: @agentclientprotocol/sdk is declared only in a private workspace manifest and never reaches the published root manifest
body:
> **Related**
>
> - **#2092** — same extension, same load failure. That report named two root causes. Its first (the `@earendil-works/pi-ai` alias prefix-match) no longer reproduces in the published tree. Its second — the undeclared dependency — is what this issue covers, and still reproduces on 0.72.0.
> - **#5** — the same publish boundary in its earlier form: the `@fusion/pi-claude-cli` workspace link reached the published manifest and npm tried to fetch it from the registry. `applyPrepackTransform` in `packages/cli/scripts/prepare-publish-manifest.mjs` deletes `@fusion/*` entries, which addresses that failure mode. This issue is the other half of the same seam: the entry is removed, but the `dependencies` it carried are not hoisted in its place.

---

## Summary

`@runfusion/fusion@0.72.0` ships `dist/pi-claude-cli/` as unbundled TypeScript. `dist/pi-claude-cli/src/acp-driver.ts:45` imports the bare specifier `@agentclientprotocol/sdk`, but that package is not declared in the published root manifest, so npm never installs it. Loading the extension fails with `MODULE_NOT_FOUND` / `Cannot find module '@agentclientprotocol/sdk'`.

This is not a regression. `@agentclientprotocol/sdk` has never appeared in any dependency block of any published version — a scan of the full packument (104 versions, all of `dependencies`, `devDependencies`, `peerDependencies`, `optionalDependencies`, `bundleDependencies`) returns 0 occurrences; the substring does not appear anywhere in the packument.

Relationship to #2092: that issue named two root causes. Its first — the pi-ai alias prefix-match mangling `@earendil-works/pi-ai/providers/all` — no longer reproduces in the currently published tree; `node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/extensions/loader.js` now carries explicit alias entries for `@earendil-works/pi-ai/providers/all` and `@mariozechner/pi-ai/providers/all`, which arrived with the 0.80.10 bump. Its second root cause — the undeclared dependency — still reproduces on 0.72.0, which is what this issue covers. Filing separately rather than reopening, since one of the two causes is genuinely resolved.

## Root cause

The dependency is declared, but in a manifest npm does not read. Inside the published tarball, `package/dist/pi-claude-cli/package.json`:

```json
{
  "name": "@fusion/pi-claude-cli",
  "version": "0.70.1",
  "type": "module",
  "private": true,
  "main": "index.ts",
  "pi": { "extensions": ["index.ts"] },
  "dependencies": { "@agentclientprotocol/sdk": "0.24.0" }
}
```

npm installs dependencies from the root manifest only. A nested `package.json` shipped as part of a dependency's file payload is inert, and this one is additionally `private: true`. In-repo the requirement is satisfied through the workspace link — `packages/cli/package.json` at main (`237d9be05f2f00b7ef5b26535f3c9532ce81f6ff`) has `"@fusion/pi-claude-cli": "workspace:*"` in `devDependencies`, and `packages/pi-claude-cli/package.json` at the same SHA is `private: true` with `"dependencies": {"@agentclientprotocol/sdk": "0.24.0"}`. `packages/cli/scripts/prepare-publish-manifest.mjs` deletes the `@fusion/*` workspace entries at prepack; nothing hoists the transitive dependency to the published root in their place. `packages/cli/package.json` at main HEAD lists 18 dependencies and `@agentclientprotocol/sdk` is not among them, so a publish from the current main would carry the same omission.

Nothing supplies it transitively. `claude-code-cli-acp@0.1.1` is declared at the published root and is ACP-adjacent, but it is a different package and has zero dependencies (`"dependencies": {}`).

Scope note, in case it saves a wider search: only `dist/pi-claude-cli/**` is affected. `dist/plugins/fusion-plugin-{acp,claude,grok,omp}-runtime/bundled.js` also reference `@agentclientprotocol/sdk`, but those are esbuild outputs with the SDK inlined — 0 bare-specifier import statements, 5 esbuild module banners each, all pointing at `@agentclientprotocol+sdk@0.24.0`. They resolve nothing at runtime and are not broken. `pi-claude-cli` is the one consumer shipped as raw `.ts` that must resolve the specifier from `node_modules`.

Also worth a deliberate decision when fixing: the private manifest pins `0.24.0`, while the registry `latest` for `@agentclientprotocol/sdk` is `1.2.1`. A naive bump to the current major may not match the driver's API.

## Reproduce

Any machine, empty directory, default npm config:

```console
$ mkdir acp-repro && cd acp-repro
$ npm init -y
$ npm i @runfusion/fusion@0.72.0
added 506 packages

$ npm ls @agentclientprotocol/sdk
acp-repro@1.0.0 <project>
`-- (empty)

$ find node_modules -type d -name '@agentclientprotocol' | wc -l
0
```

The published manifest confirms the omission directly:

```console
$ npm view @runfusion/fusion@0.72.0 dependencies --json
{
  "@earendil-works/pi-ai": "0.80.10",
  "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent": "0.80.10",
  "claude-code-cli-acp": "0.1.1",
  "dockerode": "^4.0.12",
  "electron": "^33.4.11",
  "embedded-postgres": "15.18.0-beta.17",
  "express": "^5.1.0",
  "i18next": "^26.3.1",
  "ink": "^7.0.5",
  "ink-spinner": "^5.0.0",
  "ink-text-input": "^6.0.0",
  "ioredis": "^5.6.0",
  "multer": "^2.1.1",
  "node-pty": "npm:@homebridge/node-pty-prebuilt-multiarch@^0.13.1",
  "react": "^19.2.0",
  "react-i18next": "^17.0.8",
  "ws": "^8.18.0"
}
```

Resolving exactly as the extension does, from the importing file's directory — save as `chk.cjs` in the same project and run `node chk.cjs`:

```js
const path = require('path');
const { createRequire } = require('module');
const p = path.join(process.cwd(), 'node_modules/@runfusion/fusion/dist/pi-claude-cli/src/acp-driver.ts');
const r = createRequire(p);
try { console.log('RESOLVED', r.resolve('@agentclientprotocol/sdk')); }
catch (e) { console.log('code    =', e.code); console.log('message =', e.message.split('\n')[0]); }
```

```
code    = MODULE_NOT_FOUND
message = Cannot find module '@agentclientprotocol/sdk'
```

Ruled out while narrowing this down:

- **Tarball authenticity.** The `0.72.0` tarball was downloaded fresh (21,706,529 bytes) and hashed locally: sha1 `7525c2022f635b7b9aa1ce840db595c9652e0875` and sha512 `yefVWpfl…VBgpA==` both match `dist` in the registry. Its tar index has 669 entries, matching `dist.fileCount`; `grep -ci agentclientprotocol` over that index is 0, so there is no vendored copy.
- **Local npm config.** Reproduced with `--userconfig ""` (user `.npmrc` bypassed) and with a brand-new empty `--cache` directory. Same result both times: 0 matching directories.
- **Platform.** `npm view @runfusion/fusion@0.72.0 os cpu` returns nothing for either field, and there is a single `dist.tarball` with a single `dist.integrity`, so every platform installs byte-identical content and the missing entry lives in JSON rather than in any binary. The repro above was executed on Windows only; macOS/Linux were not run.

## Suggested fix

One line — add the dependency to the published root manifest:

```diff
  // packages/cli/package.json
   "dependencies": {
+    "@agentclientprotocol/sdk": "0.24.0",
     "@earendil-works/pi-ai": "0.80.10",
```

Alternatively, and more durably, have `applyPrepackTransform` in `packages/cli/scripts/prepare-publish-manifest.mjs` hoist the `dependencies` of each `@fusion/*` workspace it deletes into the published root, so any future extension dependency is carried across the publish boundary automatically.

A cheap regression guard for this class of bug: after `npm pack`, install the tarball into an empty directory and `require.resolve` every bare specifier imported by files under `dist/pi-claude-cli/`.

## Environment

- `@runfusion/fusion` 0.72.0 (`dist-tags.latest` = 0.72.0)
- Node v24.18.0
- npm 11.16.0
- Windows 11 (win32 x64)
- Upstream reference SHA for the `main` checks above: `237d9be05f2f00b7ef5b26535f3c9532ce81f6ff` (2026-07-20T02:02:21Z)

Bug: PWA na iOS: studený start bez tokenu skončí ve smyčce „Can't reach Fusion Backend" — dialog pro vložení tokenu se nikdy nezobrazí
body:
# PWA na iOS: studený start bez tokenu skončí ve smyčce „Can't reach Fusion Backend" — dialog pro vložení tokenu se nikdy nezobrazí

**GitHub issue:** Runfusion/Fusion#TBD
**Verze Fusion:** 0.72.0 (zdrojový checkout, commit `825e6c185`)
**Oblast:** Dashboard / autentizace (PWA, vzdálený přístup přes tunel)
**Závažnost:** Vysoká — aplikaci přidanou na plochu iPhonu (a obecně jakoukoli instalovanou PWA) nelze vůbec autorizovat; jediná záchranná cesta (dialog pro vložení tokenu) kvůli race condition nikdy nenaskočí

## Shrnutí

Dashboard zpřístupněný přes Remote Access (Tailscale Funnel) funguje v mobilním Safari
správně — token přiteče přes `?token=...` v přihlašovací URL a uloží se do localStorage.
Po **Přidat na plochu** na iOS ale instalovaná webová aplikace:

- startuje vždy na `start_url: "/"` z PWA manifestu — tedy **bez tokenu v URL**,
- běží v **izolovaném úložišti** (localStorage se Safari nesdílí), takže žádný token nemá,
- a místo dialogu pro vložení tokenu (`AuthTokenRecoveryDialog`) zobrazí jen chybovou
  stránku **„Can't reach Fusion Backend … Error: Unauthorized"** s tlačítkem
  **Retry Connection**, které viditelně nic nedělá.

Do izolovaného úložiště PWA se token žádnou jinou cestou dostat nedá, takže instalovaná
aplikace je trvale nepoužitelná.

## Root cause — jednorázově latchovaná recovery událost prohraje závod s mount-em posluchače

Vše v `packages/dashboard/app` (0.72.0):

- **Detekce:** patchovaný `fetch` (`auth.ts`) na same-origin `/api/*` odpověď 401 s přesným
  payloadem `{ error: "Unauthorized", message: "Valid bearer token required" }`
  (vrací `packages/dashboard/src/auth-middleware.ts:157`) zavolá
  `emitDaemonAuthRecoverySignal()` (`auth.ts:213`), který vystřelí událost
  `fn:auth-token-recovery-required`.
- **Latch:** událost se vystřelí **pouze jednou** — `auth.ts:214` ji při
  `daemonAuthFailureSignaled === true` zahodí a `auth.ts:218` latch nastaví. Latch se
  resetuje jen v `setAuthToken`/`clearAuthToken` (`auth.ts:116`, `auth.ts:123`), které se
  bez tokenu nikdy nezavolají.
- **Posluchač:** `useAuthTokenRecovery` (`hooks/useAuthTokenRecovery.ts`) registruje
  posluchač události až v `useEffect` uvnitř `AppInner` a teprve pak otevře
  `AuthTokenRecoveryDialog` (`App.tsx:1998`).
- **Závod:** při studeném startu bez tokenu první `/api/*` požadavek vrátí 401 dřív, než
  se posluchač zaregistruje. Jednorázová událost zazní do prázdna, latch se sepne — a
  **Retry Connection** pak dostává další 401, ale událost se už nikdy znovu nevyšle.
  Uživatel zůstane trvale na `BackendConnectionErrorPage` bez možnosti token vložit.

V Safari se bug maskuje tím, že token přichází přes `?token=...`; postižené jsou všechny
instalované PWA (iOS „Přidat na plochu", Android „Instalovat aplikaci") a jakýkoli čistý
prohlížeč otevřený na holé URL bez tokenu.

## Reprodukce

1. Spusťte `fn dashboard` s aktivní bearer-token autentizací a zpřístupněte ho přes
   Remote Access (např. Tailscale Funnel).
2. Na iPhonu otevřete vygenerovanou přihlašovací URL v Safari (funguje), poté
   **Sdílet → Přidat na plochu**.
3. Otevřete aplikaci **z plochy**: zobrazí se „Can't reach Fusion Backend …
   Error: Unauthorized".
4. Tlačítko **Retry Connection** opakuje 401, ale dialog pro vložení tokenu se nikdy
   neotevře — aplikace je trvale zaseknutá.
5. Ekvivalentní repro bez iPhonu: otevřete dashboard v čistém prohlížečovém profilu na
   holé URL (bez `?token=`).

## Očekávané chování

Nepřihlášený studený start nabídne vložení tokenu — buď se `AuthTokenRecoveryDialog`
otevře spolehlivě i pro 401, které proběhlo před mount-em posluchače, nebo
`BackendConnectionErrorPage` u chyby Unauthorized zobrazí pole pro token přímo.

## Náměty na opravu

- Nespoléhat na jednorázovou událost: držet stav „daemon auth failure" dotazovatelně
  (např. getter v `auth.ts`) a v `useAuthTokenRecovery` ho přečíst i při mountu; a/nebo
  latch resetovat při retry, aby další 401 událost vyslal znovu.
- Alternativně/navíc: `BackendConnectionErrorPage` při `Error: Unauthorized` rovnou
  renderovat vstup pro token (stejná logika jako `AuthTokenRecoveryDialog`).

## Workaround

Žádný použitelný neexistuje. Izolované úložiště instalované PWA nelze z Safari naplnit a
`start_url` z manifestu (`packages/dashboard/app/public/manifest.json:5`) token v URL
neumožňuje. Jediná možnost je PWA nepoužívat a zůstat u záložky v Safari otevírané přes
tokenizovanou přihlašovací URL.

title: Embedded PostgreSQL cluster created with the OS-locale encoding (WIN1254) instead of UTF-8 on non-UTF-8 Windows locales - dashboard crash-loops during migration (0.71.0; #2284 mmap fix confirmed)
body:
## First: #2284 (embedded Postgres `shared_memory_type=mmap` on Windows) is fixed and confirmed on 0.71.0

Answering the earlier maintainer question - yes, the Windows mmap `FATAL` from #2284 is resolved in `@runfusion/fusion@0.71.0` (the platform-aware fix from commit `8517a5d`, shipped in v0.70.2). Verified in the installed `dist/bin.js`:

- `defaultEmbeddedPostgresFlagsFor` (~line 46013) returns:

```js
platform === "win32" ? [] : ["-c", "shared_memory_type=mmap"]
```

- At runtime, embedded PostgreSQL now starts on Windows and reaches `database system is ready to accept connections`; the previous mmap `FATAL` no longer occurs.

Upgrading to 0.71.0 lets me drop the local dist patch I was carrying for #2284. Thanks for the fix.

However, once Postgres starts, a **separate** problem keeps the default embedded backend from booting the dashboard on my machine. Filing it below.

---

## New issue: embedded cluster is created with the OS-locale encoding (WIN1254), but Fusion requires UTF-8

**Environment (short):** Windows 11, Node v24.18.0, `@runfusion/fusion@0.71.0`, default embedded PostgreSQL backend (bundled `@embedded-postgres/windows-x64` → PostgreSQL 15.18), `DATABASE_URL` unset. System regional locale is Turkish (`Turkish_Türkiye.1254` / Windows code page WIN1254) - the ordinary default on Turkish Windows, not a special configuration. (Full details at the bottom.)

### Reproduction

1. On a Windows machine whose system locale is non-UTF-8 (e.g. Turkish / WIN1254, with the "Beta: Use Unicode UTF-8 for worldwide language support" option **off** - the default), install `@runfusion/fusion@0.71.0`.
2. Leave `DATABASE_URL` unset so the default embedded PostgreSQL backend is used.
3. Start Fusion.
4. Observe: PostgreSQL starts, but the dashboard crash-loops during schema/migration initialization and the supervisor gives up.

### Symptom

After Postgres starts, the dashboard crash-loops during schema/migration init until the supervisor gives up:

```
dashboard exited unexpectedly 4 times. Giving up.
```

The recurring server-side error (observed 8× in a single run):

```
ERROR:  character with byte sequence 0xe2 0x86 0x92 in encoding "UTF8" has no equivalent in encoding "WIN1254"
```

`0xe2 0x86 0x92` is the UTF-8 encoding of `U+2192` (`→`), which appears in the first schema migration.

### Root cause

Fusion invokes `initdb` for the embedded cluster **without `--encoding`**, so on a non-UTF-8 Windows locale `initdb` inherits the OS locale encoding (here WIN1254). Meanwhile Fusion connects with `client_encoding: "UTF8"` and ships migrations containing UTF-8 characters (`→`). PostgreSQL must then convert that UTF-8 data to the WIN1254 database encoding for storage, and characters with no WIN1254 equivalent fail. Fusion requires a UTF-8-capable database but never ensures one is created at cluster-creation time.

This is a two-factor interaction: Fusion's omitted `--encoding` flag **and** a non-UTF-8 OS locale. The Turkish locale is the necessary trigger, not the fault - the fault is that Fusion doesn't guarantee the UTF-8 cluster its own code requires. The omitted flag is itself platform-independent; it simply doesn't surface where the OS locale is already UTF-8 (typical Linux/macOS).

### Evidence

**1. The real cluster is WIN1254.** Querying the actual `~/.fusion` embedded cluster (via `postgres --single`), every database is WIN1254 with a Turkish collation:

```
 datname   | encoding | datcollate
-----------+----------+----------------------
 fusion    | WIN1254  | Turkish_Türkiye.1254
 postgres  | WIN1254  | Turkish_Türkiye.1254
 template0 | WIN1254  | Turkish_Türkiye.1254
 template1 | WIN1254  | Turkish_Türkiye.1254
```

**2. Fusion demands UTF-8 but never requests it at creation.** In the shipped 0.71.0 dist there is **no** `--encoding` / `--locale` / `--lc-*` flag anywhere in the `initdb` path:

- `initdbFlags: opts.initdbFlags ?? []` (~line 46261) - defaults to an empty array
- `initdbFlags: [...this.options.initdbFlags]` (~line 46411) - passed through unchanged
- `client_encoding: "UTF8"` (~line 43503) - the client connection sets UTF-8

So Fusion requires a UTF-8-capable database but does not guarantee one is created.

**3. Counterfactual** (bundled `initdb`, fresh throwaway clusters on the same machine, real data untouched) - isolates the single omitted flag:

```
# same machine, same Turkish OS locale

# WITHOUT --encoding (what Fusion does) -> reproduces the real cluster exactly
initdb <datadir>
  => encoding = WIN1254,  collate = Turkish_Türkiye.1254

# WITH the flag -> UTF-8 cluster
initdb --encoding=UTF8 --locale=C <datadir>
  => encoding = UTF8,     collate = C
```

**4. User-side ruled out.** No `LANG` / `LC_*` / `PGCLIENTENCODING` environment variables are set, and `~/.fusion/config.json` and `settings.json` contain no encoding or locale setting. The WIN1254 comes solely from the OS regional default that `initdb` inherits because Fusion does not override it - this is plain default Turkish Windows, with no opt-in configuration.

### Impact / scope

On a non-UTF-8 Windows locale, the default embedded PostgreSQL backend cannot complete schema setup, so the dashboard never boots. With no SQLite opt-out, the only current escape is pointing `DATABASE_URL` at an external UTF-8 server.

The confirmed case is Turkish / WIN1254. The root cause (omitted `--encoding`) is not specific to Turkish - it will surface on any Windows system whose active code page cannot represent the characters Fusion writes. I have not tested other locales, but `→` is also absent from Windows-1252, so a default Western/US Windows box running a legacy (non-UTF-8) active code page is a plausible **(untested)** candidate. A maintainer testing with the UTF-8 beta enabled (active code page 65001) will get a UTF-8 cluster and will **not** reproduce - so please check the active code page when attempting a repro.

### Secondary side-effect (observed, not a separately confirmed bug)

When the dashboard crashes mid-init, an embedded postmaster can be left behind and/or a stale `postmaster.pid` remains, so a subsequent start can fail with:

```
FATAL:  lock file "postmaster.pid" already exists
```

…until the stale file is removed. I'm reporting this only as an observation of a generic crash-during-init artifact, not asserting that the encoding failure directly causes it. It reads as a downstream consequence of the crash-loop rather than an independent defect, so I've kept it here rather than opening a separate issue.

### Suggested fix

Specify the encoding when creating the embedded cluster, so the database encoding matches the `client_encoding=UTF8` Fusion already uses. Because `client_encoding` is always UTF-8 on every platform, this should be applied **unconditionally** (unlike the mmap flag, which is legitimately platform-specific - please don't gate the encoding to Windows only):

```
initdb --encoding=UTF8 --locale=C   # plus existing flags
```

Notes for reviewers:

- `--encoding=UTF8` should be paired with a UTF-8-compatible locale (`--locale=C`, or a `*.UTF-8` locale). On a non-UTF-8 system `initdb` derives its target encoding from the inherited locale, so the encoding and locale need to be set together - which is why the counterfactual above used `--encoding=UTF8 --locale=C`.
- `--locale=C` gives deterministic byte-order collation rather than locale-aware sorting (e.g. Turkish dotted/dotless `i`). For a managed embedded app DB this is usually an acceptable, predictable tradeoff; a `*.UTF-8` locale is an alternative if locale-aware sort matters, and per-column `COLLATE` remains available.
- **Not retroactive:** a database's encoding cannot be changed in place (there is no `ALTER DATABASE … SET ENCODING`), so this only fixes newly created clusters. Existing WIN1254 clusters must be re-initialized - the simplest path for an already-affected user is to delete the `~/.fusion` embedded cluster and let Fusion recreate it (any half-migrated data from a crashed init is unusable anyway). Worth calling out as a known upgrade cost so the fix isn't read as an instant unbreak for already-affected installs.

### Environment (full)

- `@runfusion/fusion` 0.71.0, embedded PostgreSQL 15.18 (bundled `@embedded-postgres/windows-x64`)
- Windows 11, Node v24.18.0, `process.platform === "win32"`
- System locale: `Turkish_Türkiye.1254` (active code page WIN1254), UTF-8 beta not enabled
- `DATABASE_URL` unset; no `LANG` / `LC_*` / `PGCLIENTENCODING` set; no encoding/locale keys in `~/.fusion/config.json` or `settings.json`

title: Embedded PostgreSQL fails to boot on Windows in 0.70.0: unconditional shared_memory_type=mmap flag is invalid on win32
body:
## Summary

On Windows, `fn` fails to start in **@runfusion/fusion 0.70.0** because Fusion always passes `-c shared_memory_type=mmap` to the embedded PostgreSQL postmaster. On Windows, PostgreSQL only accepts `shared_memory_type=windows`, so the postmaster aborts during startup on every attempt.

The flag comes from a hard-coded constant in the published bundle (identical for every install), and the Windows `shared_memory_type` constraint is universal, so this appears to affect **every Windows user on 0.70.0 that relies on the embedded PostgreSQL backend** — which is the default local backend when `DATABASE_URL` is unset. With the SQLite opt-out removed in this release, that leaves affected users with no working local backend until they point `DATABASE_URL` at an external server (see Workarounds).

## Environment

- Fusion: `@runfusion/fusion` **0.70.0**
- OS: Windows 11 (`process.platform === "win32"`, `process.arch === "x64"`)
- Node: v24.18.0
- Backend: embedded PostgreSQL (default local backend, `DATABASE_URL` unset)
- Bundled binary: `@embedded-postgres/windows-x64` → PostgreSQL 15.18

## Symptom

Running `fn` fails; the dashboard supervisor retries (`attempt 1/4 ...`) and the embedded postmaster dies on every attempt. Engine log:

```
[startup-factory] starting embedded PostgreSQL (data dir C:\Users\<user>\.fusion\embedded-postgres\default)
[startup-factory] embedded postgres: existing data directory ..., reusing without initdb
FATAL:  invalid value for parameter "shared_memory_type": "mmap"
HINT:  Available values: windows.
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. On Windows, install `@runfusion/fusion@0.70.0`.
2. Leave `DATABASE_URL` unset (so the default embedded PostgreSQL backend is used).
3. Run `fn`.

Result: the supervisor retries (`attempt 1/4 ...`) and the embedded postmaster exits every time with the `FATAL` / `HINT` shown above.

This is **not** tied to a stale data directory. Failure occurs both with an existing data dir (the user log above shows `reusing without initdb`) and with a freshly initialized cluster (the isolated repro below uses a brand-new throwaway cluster and still fails). Deleting `~/.fusion` does not help.

## Isolated reproduction

Using the **bundled** PostgreSQL 15.18 binary against a fresh throwaway cluster (the real `~/.fusion` data is untouched), only the flag value differs between the two outcomes:

```sh
# FAILS — reproduces the exact FATAL/HINT above
postgres -D <tmp_cluster> -c shared_memory_type=mmap
# FATAL:  invalid value for parameter "shared_memory_type": "mmap"
# HINT:  Available values: windows.

# STARTS — server comes up normally
postgres -D <tmp_cluster> -c shared_memory_type=windows
# ... database system is ready to accept connections
```

On Windows, the `shared_memory_type` GUC only accepts `windows`; `mmap` and `sysv` are non-Windows values. `mmap` is therefore categorically invalid on Windows and the server aborts during GUC startup.

## Root cause

The default flags constant is defined **unconditionally** (no platform guard) in the shipped bundle:

```js
// dist/bin.js (~line 46167) and dist/extension.js (~line 46165)
DEFAULT_EMBEDDED_POSTGRES_FLAGS = ["-c", "shared_memory_type=mmap"]
```

It is merged into the postmaster args in the `EmbeddedPostgresLifecycle` constructor:

```js
// ~line 46210
postgresFlags: [...DEFAULT_EMBEDDED_POSTGRES_FLAGS, ...opts.postgresFlags ?? []]
```

These flags are sanitized via `sanitizePostgresFlags` (~line 45528) and then passed to the spawned `postgres` process. As a result, Fusion hands `-c shared_memory_type=mmap` to the postmaster on **every** platform, including win32 where the value is invalid.

> The `~line` numbers are offsets into the minified/bundled `dist` files and may drift between installs; the durable anchors are the symbol names `DEFAULT_EMBEDDED_POSTGRES_FLAGS` and `sanitizePostgresFlags`. Since `dist` is generated, the platform guard should be applied in the corresponding source (TypeScript) definition of `DEFAULT_EMBEDDED_POSTGRES_FLAGS`, then rebuilt.

## Ruled-out user-side causes

The invalid value does **not** originate from the user environment or config. All of the following were verified read-only:

- No `PG*` / `POSTGRES*` / `DATABASE*` environment variables set; `PGOPTIONS` is empty in Process, User, and Machine scopes.
- No environment variable contains `mmap`.
- `~/.fusion/config.json` and `settings.json` contain no `postgresFlags`, `shared_memory`, `mmap`, or `DATABASE_URL`.
- The cluster `postgresql.conf` has `shared_memory_type` only as a **comment** (not active); `postgresql.auto.conf` is empty.

The value comes solely from the hard-coded `DEFAULT_EMBEDDED_POSTGRES_FLAGS` constant described above.

## Impact / scope

- The constant is a fixed string in the published npm package (identical for every install), and the Windows `shared_memory_type` constraint is universal → the embedded PostgreSQL backend cannot boot on any Windows install of 0.70.0.
- The embedded PostgreSQL backend is the default local backend when `DATABASE_URL` is unset.
- The SQLite opt-out has been removed. The shipped code states: *"The SQLite opt-out has been removed. Unset FUSION_NO_EMBEDDED_PG and use embedded PostgreSQL, or configure DATABASE_URL."* (dist ~lines 59453 / 59602). So there is no built-in SQLite fallback to fall back to.

## Workarounds for affected users

Until a fix ships, either:

1. **Point `DATABASE_URL` at an external PostgreSQL server.** This bypasses the embedded backend entirely and is the least invasive option.
2. **Patch the shipped constant locally.** Change `DEFAULT_EMBEDDED_POSTGRES_FLAGS` in both `dist/bin.js` and `dist/extension.js` to add a `win32` guard (see Suggested fix). Applying exactly this guard was verified to let the embedded PostgreSQL boot normally. Note this is overwritten on reinstall/upgrade.

Note: **`FUSION_NO_EMBEDDED_PG` is not a workaround.** The SQLite opt-out it used to gate has been removed, so unsetting/setting it does not restore a working local backend.

## Likely origin (hypothesis)

This **appears** to be a leak from a change intended to make embedded PostgreSQL boot on Linux/POSIX hosts with a small (~64MB) `/dev/shm` — a shared-memory workaround that wants `mmap` — applied globally without excluding `win32`. This is a hypothesis, not confirmed; it is offered only to help locate the change, not as an established fact.

## Suggested fix

Make the default platform-aware so Windows never receives `mmap`:

```js
const DEFAULT_EMBEDDED_POSTGRES_FLAGS =
  process.platform === "win32" ? [] : ["-c", "shared_memory_type=mmap"];
// (alternatively, set ["-c", "shared_memory_type=windows"] on win32)
```

Apply this in source and rebuild so both `dist/bin.js` and `dist/extension.js` pick it up. This guard was verified locally to let the embedded PostgreSQL boot normally on Windows.

## Open items (for the maintainers)

- **Last-known-good / regression-introducing version:** not determined on the reporting side, so not stated here to avoid guessing — worth pinning down which release first shipped the unconditional `mmap` flag.
- **Duplicate check:** as of filing, searching the issue tracker for `shared_memory_type`, `mmap`, and `embedded postgres windows` returned no matching issues, and no fixing commit was found (latest release is still 0.70.0). Please re-verify against current tracker state.

title: npm install issue: npm install -g @runfusion/fusion breaks due to unpinned caret ranges on @earendil-works/pi-* packages
body:
`npm install -g @runfusion/fusion` is unreliable because `package.json` uses caret ranges on `@earendil-works/pi-*` packages that are meant to be version-locked as a set from their own monorepo (evidenced by a committed `pnpm-lock.yaml`). `npm install -g` doesn't respect that lockfile, so it re-resolves each range independently and can produce a mismatched set. 

Reproduced: `pi-coding-agent@0.80.10` missing `AuthStorage` export that `dist/bin.js` imports directly (`SyntaxError: The requested module '@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent' does not provide an export named 'AuthStorage'`); forcing `pi-coding-agent` back to `0.80.6` alone then breaks instead on `pi-ai`'s `getOAuthProvider` export. Confirmed both `0.57.0` and `0.60.0` use unpinned caret ranges. Building from source via `pnpm install` (respects lockfile) works cleanly. Suggest pinning exact versions in `package.json`, or documenting build-from-source as the reliable install path until upstream (`pi-mono`) stabilizes. Node v25.9.0, npm 11.12.1, macOS 26.5.2, Apple Silicon.

title: mDNS/hostname issue: fn dashboard triggers repeated macOS local hostname renames via _fusion._tcp rebroadcast (spurious multicast conflict)
body:
Running Fusion's daemon (`fn dashboard`) causes macOS to repeatedly rename its own local hostname (`MacHostname` → `MacHostname-2` → `MacHostname-3`, incrementing each run/rebroadcast).

Repro: Run `fn dashboard`, watch `tcpdump -i en0 -n port 5353` and `log stream --predicate 'process == "mDNSResponder"'` concurrently. ~1.2–1.4s after Fusion's own `_fusion._tcp` PTR/SRV/TXT rebroadcast, macOS renames the local hostname and mDNSResponder logs `mDNSCoreReceiveResponse: ... possibly spurious multicast conflict ... will deregister ... due to multicast conflict` on `en0`.

Evidence: Across two independent captures, every mDNS record involved (old and new hostname) originates from the Mac's own IP, no second device on the network. The rename tracks tightly with Fusion's own rebroadcast timing specifically; concurrent unrelated mDNS traffic (a phone's `_companion-link`/`_asquic` Continuity chatter) is present continuously throughout both captures but shows no similar spike-correlation with the rename moments. We have not run a fully isolated test with AirDrop/Handoff disabled, so we can't rule out an interaction with a macOS Continuity bug with 100% certainty, but the self-originated records plus the tight, repeatable timing to Fusion's rebroadcast make Fusion's own `_fusion._tcp` announcement the leading suspect.

Suspect Fusion's periodic re-announcement of its `_fusion._tcp` service isn't using correct mDNS probe/defend semantics, causing macOS to interpret its own repeated re-broadcast as a naming conflict.

Reproduced on both the broken npm-global install and a clean source build (`v0.60.0`, `pnpm build:exe)`, so this isn't a symptom of the packaging/caret-range issue (separate report).

Also has no user-facing opt-out, full settings export and `docs/settings-reference.md` checked, no `discoveryEnabled`/`meshEnabled`/similar key exists. Requesting either a probe/defend fix, an opt-out toggle for LAN auto-discovery, or both.

Environment: macOS 26.5.2, Apple Silicon, Node v25.9.0, npm 11.12.1, Fusion v0.60.0.

title: Compression des logs et globalement du dossier lié à une tâche lors de l'archivage de celle-ci.
body:
Fusion enregistre les logs des tâches dans un dossier sur le filesystem. Après des milliers de tâches, la pression sur l'espace disque de l'utilisateur peut être conséquent.
Pour résoudre ce problème, je propose qu'on compresse le dossier du numéro de la tâche lorsque l'on archive celle-ci dans l'interface de fusion après qu'elle soit "done".
Trouve le meilleur moyen de compression pour le perdre aucune information, mais que ce soit rapide à archiver et à desarchiver si l'utilisateur décide de desarchiver une tâche.

Estimer le gain de place en pourcentage de la compression par rapport à la non compression. Si le gain obtenu n'est pas au delà des 85/95% de compression, alors ne pas mettre en place cette modification et envoyer un message dans la messagerie du Dashboard Fusion pour indiquer les tradeoffs et les solutions alternatives proposées.

Anticiper et voir aussi comment ça se passe actuellement et comment ça va se passer lorsqu'on créer une tâche et que celle-ci regarde les tâches qui ont déjà été réalisées pour savoir comment les tâches archivées sont lues et prises en compte afin de ne pas entraver le fonctionnement habituel de Fusion lors de la préparation d'une tâche.

title: v0.6: Worktree acquisition fails with 'fatal: a branch named fusion/next-<id> already exists'
body:
## Summary
In Fusion v0.6, when a task re-enters execution and the engine tries to acquire a new worktree, it runs `git worktree add -b "fusion/next-<id>" ...`. If a branch with that exact name already exists from a previous run (orphaned branch, phantom-worktree recovery, or an earlier task that was reset), the acquisition fails three times and the task stalls. This is a **separate but related** failure mode from the broader task-regression issue described in #2124.

## Related issues
- #2124 — tasks regress from in-progress to planning/todo due to provider/state failures.
- #2125 — `triageDefaultWorkflowId` enum rejects custom workflow IDs.
- #2126 — Dashboard/API-created tasks frequently have no agent assigned.

This issue is **not a duplicate** of the above; it is a distinct root cause in the worktree-acquisition path that produces the same visible symptom (tasks stuck and not moving).

## Observed error
```
Worktree acquisition failed after 3 heartbeat attempts for branch "fusion/next-1378": Command failed: git worktree add -b "fusion/next-1378" ".../.worktrees/<name>" "main"
Preparing worktree (new branch 'fusion/next-1378')
fatal: a branch named 'fusion/next-1378' already exists
```

## Reproduction
1. A task is created and the engine checks out a worktree on branch `fusion/next-XXXX`.
2. The worktree is later removed (e.g., engine reset, phantom-worktree cleanup, or manual intervention), but the branch ref is left behind.
3. The task re-enters the execution queue.
4. The engine attempts `git worktree add -b "fusion/next-XXXX"` and fails because the branch exists.
5. After 3 failures, the task is marked failed/paused and stops progressing.

## Expected behavior
When the target branch already exists, the engine should either:
- Reuse the existing branch (e.g., `git worktree add --checkout <path> <branch>`), or
- Rebase/reset the existing branch onto `main` before creating the worktree, or
- Clean up the orphaned branch before creating a new one.

It should not fail permanently with a deterministic branch-name collision.

## Impact
- Tasks that are otherwise ready to execute (or even already partly done) get stuck indefinitely.
- It creates duplicate manual cleanup work for operators.
- It can also lead to branch contamination, where one task's branch accumulates commits from multiple tasks because the engine sometimes acquires the next worktree from the previous worktree's checked-out branch rather than from `main`.

## Workaround
We have implemented a local repair script (`scripts/fusion_worktree_collision_repair.py`) that detects the signature in `.fusion/fusion.db`, safely removes merged branches, renames unmerged branches to recovery backups, removes orphan worktree directories, and re-queues the affected tasks. We will disable this script once the engine handles branch collisions natively.

## Environment
- Fusion v0.6
- Windows (observed at `C:\Users\...\NextGenEHS`)
- Git worktrees under `.worktrees\<random-name>`

title: Améliorer le système de détection de duplication de tache
body:
Actuellement quand le système estime qu'une tâche est une tâche dupliquée, il supprime tâche en question.

Au lieu de cela, il faut bloquer la tâche dans un status adapté qui nécessite une décision d'utilisateur dans la tâche entre supprimer ou continuer malgré tout.

Ajouter également dans les paramètres la possibilité de définir un comportement automatique entre la suppression et le fait de garder la tâche malgré la duplication.

Afin de pouvoir correctement faire le bon choix, l'interface qui demande si on maintient ou supprime la tâche doit avoir un lien vers la tâche dupliquée pour pouvoir la consulter avant de faire notre choix.
