title: Changement de configuration pour le fallback des modèles AI
body:
Au lieu d'avoir 1 seul paramètre général de fallback (c'est à dire que si n'importe quel agent qui utilise le modèle primaire tombe sur une erreur il utilise ce modèle fallback général), au lieu de ça, permettre de définir le modèle fallback pour chaque type d'agent (reviewer, merger etc.) car nous ne voulons pas forcément que le même modèle de fallback soit utilisé pour tous les types d'agents.

De plus, dans le travail d'une tâche, si le modèle primaire entre en erreur, et que le modèle de fallback aussi entre en erreur, faire en sorte que la dernière tentative réutilise le modèle primaire au lieu de boucler sur le fallback. Si les "retry" primaire + fallback + primaire échoue, afficher explicitement le problème à l'utilisateur pour une résolution manuelle du problème en bloquant la tâche et permettant à l'utilisateur de réessayer quand il aura résolu le problème indiqué.

title: [Feature] In-app agentic bug/feedback/idea/help that opens repo issues
body:
**Labels:** feature-request · community · dogfooding · Wave 3

## Summary
Add four in-app actions — **Bug / Feedback / Idea / Help** — that are themselves **agentic**: instead of opening a text box, each one gathers relevant context, structures it into a well-formed report, checks for duplicates, and (in a user-chosen mode) files it directly as a repo issue or discussion.

## Problem
Today, reporting a bug or requesting a feature means leaving the product, remembering the relevant context unaided, and hand-writing an issue from scratch. There is no in-product path from "something's wrong / here's an idea" to a well-formed, actionable report — and no equivalent for a quick help question. The result is that most friction and most ideas never get captured at all; only the most motivated users write anything down, and what they write is usually thinner than what actually happened. This very feedback pack — hours of manually reconstructing context, structuring it, and cross-referencing it — is the proof: it is exactly the work this feature should do automatically.

## Proposed capability

### Four actions, one underlying pipeline
- **Bug** — something broke or misbehaved.
- **Feedback** — general friction, praise, or a complaint that isn't quite a bug or a feature ask.
- **Idea** — a feature request or improvement suggestion.
- **Help** — "how do I do X" — routes first to a self-check against docs/known capabilities (see FR-11) before ever becoming an issue.

All four share one agentic pipeline: **gather → structure → scrub → dedupe → file.**

### The agentic pipeline
- **Gather:** pull relevant context automatically — recent error/log lines, the agent/task involved, current mode/settings, and (where applicable) a screenshot or short activity trace — rather than relying on the user to describe everything from memory.
- **Structure:** compile it into a standard report shape (summary, repro/context, expected vs. actual, environment) instead of a raw paragraph — the same shift from "raw complaint" to "structured, actionable input" that this pack itself demonstrates by hand.
- **Scrub:** strip identifying details (paths, project names, personal data) before anything leaves the product — non-negotiable, not a mode.
- **Dedupe:** check the report against existing open issues/discussions (and the public roadmap, FR-30) first, so a common complaint strengthens an existing thread with a 👍 and a data point instead of spawning a duplicate.
- **File:** open the resulting issue or discussion in the repo.

### Draft-for-review vs. auto-file — a selectable mode
Whether the agent **files directly** or **shows the user a draft to approve/edit first** is a selectable mode, set at onboarding (or per-action-type) — some users want zero friction, others want to see exactly what leaves the product before it does. Either way, the user always sees the final report once it's filed.

## Prior-art (inspiration)
- [Aider](https://github.com/Aider-AI/aider) — on an uncaught error, offers to open a prefilled GitHub issue with a scrubbed traceback and environment details, asking for confirmation before it's sent: exactly the "gather + scrub + confirm" shape this feature needs.
- [GitHub Desktop](https://github.com/desktop/desktop) — its built-in "Report an issue" flow pre-populates a structured GitHub issue template from app context, so the user edits a draft rather than starting from a blank box.
- [Sentry](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry) — its user-feedback widget automatically attaches session/environment context to a report rather than asking the user to reconstruct it in prose.

## Why now
It closes the loop between using Fusion and improving it, at basically no cost to the user — capture happens where friction actually occurs, not hours later in a separate document. And a structured, agent-authored, deduped report (as this pack demonstrates by hand) is far more actionable for maintainers than a raw complaint or a wishlist buried in a chat log.

title: [Feature] Expand the agent tool surface so the full loop runs from chat (ideation → research → roadmap → plan → exec → E2E)
body:
**Labels:** feature-request · tooling · orchestration · Wave 3

## Summary
Broaden the agent-facing tool surface (the `fn_*` family) so a user can drive the **entire build loop from a single chat** — ideation, research, roadmap building, task creation/planning/execution, and E2E verification — without ever dropping to manual steps the agents simply can't reach. And once that surface exists, make sure work actually **runs in parallel** across it instead of draining through one workflow at a time.

## Problem
The tool surface is too narrow, and the gap shows up exactly where the product promises to be seamless. A concrete, lived sequence: research could not be woven into the system at all — there was no path from *ideation → research → roadmap → execution*, so research output stayed a dead document. Everything that did happen ran through a single workflow; a mission got designed, but the tasks under it never actually executed. The system was never really parallel — even when work was structured to run on multiple tracks, it processed one task at a time (or stalled). The run burned a large amount of tokens and produced no usable product at the end. And in the gap between "mission designed" and "tasks executed," an agent picked up unrelated, self-invented work instead of waiting or escalating (the off-mission-task pattern covered by FR-02) — because idle time with a narrow tool surface becomes idle time an agent fills with whatever it can reach.

The pattern underneath all of this: chat is supposed to be the control surface for the whole loop, but the tools available to agents only cover a fraction of it. Every step the tool surface doesn't reach becomes a step the human has to do by hand, or a step an agent quietly skips, fakes, or replaces with something else.

## Proposed capability

### One tool surface, covering the whole loop
- A genuinely broad `fn_*` tool surface that lets an agent invoke, as first-class tool calls, every stage of the loop: ideation (diverge/converge, FR-14), research (FR-15's pipeline), roadmap read/write (Sprint → Mission → Milestone → Task), task creation/planning/assignment, execution, and E2E verification.
- This is the same principle as **managers operating native features through real tools rather than prompt instructions** (FR-07) — FR-08 is that principle applied to the *entire* build loop, not just the roadmap/eval slice managers touch. Native features (research, roadmap, evals) stop being things an agent can only describe and become things an agent can actually call.

### Dual-access, not tool-surface-as-afterthought
- Every capability exposed as a tool is also directly usable by the human from its own UI (the dual-access principle) — the tool surface is the *same* operation the Command Center exposes, not a parallel, weaker path. That's what makes "just ask" a real substitute for clicking through the UI instead of a narrower shortcut.

### Real parallelism, not just tool breadth
- A wider tool surface only closes half the gap: work also needs to actually **run concurrently** once it's structured to. Missions/sprints designed to run in parallel (FR-14's hierarchy) should execute in parallel — not serialize silently through a single workflow while the roadmap says otherwise.
- Pairs with symbol-level locking for safe concurrent edits (FR-48) so parallelism doesn't trade correctness for throughput.

### Guardrails come with the wider surface
- A broader tool surface increases the surface area for off-mission action, so it ships alongside — not instead of — the kernel-level admission gate (FR-02): a task only runs if it traces to an approved mission. More tools for agents to use is only safe if there's a deterministic check on *which* work they're allowed to pick up with them.
- Completed work reconciles back to mission/roadmap state automatically (FR-37) rather than leaving the human to notice a stale board after the fact.

## Prior-art (inspiration)
- [loki-mode](https://github.com/asklokesh/loki-mode) — the orchestrator exposes itself as an MCP server with a broad tool set (34 tools) spanning the build loop, so an external agent (or the user, via chat) can drive the whole thing rather than being limited to a handful of narrow actions.
- [OpenGoat](https://github.com/marian2js/opengoat) — a CEO→Manager→IC hierarchy where each layer has real tool-level authority over the tree beneath it, the same shape FR-08 wants applied across ideation → research → roadmap → execution.
- [Paperclip](https://github.com/paperclipai/paperclip) — goal-lineage as something the org's own agents read and write through tools, not just a field the UI displays — the same "tools over dashboards" shift this request is asking for.

## Why now
End-to-end-from-chat is the core promise of an agentic build system — the whole pitch collapses if half the loop still requires manual intervention. Today's gaps force exactly that: research that never reaches the roadmap, missions that don't translate into executing tasks, no real parallelism even when the plan calls for it, and idle agents filling the silence with self-invented work. Closing the tool surface — and making sure work actually runs across it in parallel, safely — is what turns "an agent that can describe the loop" into "an agent that runs it."

title: [Bug] fn_send_message stores and reports success for messages to nonexistent recipient ids; they are silently lost
body:
# fn_send_message stores and reports success for messages to nonexistent recipient ids; they are silently lost

## Summary

`fn_send_message` performs no recipient-existence check. A message addressed to an agent id that does not exist is stored, reported to the sender as "Message sent," and then never delivered, read, or flagged. The sender gets false delivery confidence with no operator- or sender-visible signal.

Severity is genuinely low, and the proximate trigger is an agent inventing a recipient id / role-name (an LLM behavior). But the strictly Fusion-code defect — affirmatively reporting delivery success for an undeliverable message, with no validation and no dead-letter — is real and config-independent.

## Expected vs Actual

- **Expected:** Sending to a nonexistent agent id returns an error (or at least a delivery warning), so the sender knows the message was not deliverable.
- **Actual:** The row is inserted, the tool returns `Message sent to <to_id> (ID: ...)`, the wake hook silently no-ops because the recipient is unknown, and nothing ever reads the orphaned row. It stays unread forever with no error or warning.

## Root cause (exact locations, dist/bin.js)

Three stages, none validate the recipient:
- `145292` — `const recipient = recipientType === "user" ? normalizeMessageParticipant(params.to_id, recipientType) : { id: params.to_id, type: recipientType };` — for agent recipients the id is taken verbatim from `params.to_id` with no lookup against the agents table. (The only pre-send validations are empty-content, length, and non-empty `reply_to` — never recipient identity.)
- `145328` — on a successful store the tool returns `Message sent to ${params.to_id} (ID: ${result.value.id})` regardless of whether the recipient exists — the false-success surface.
- `33682` — `MessageStore.sendMessage` validates only metadata, then unconditionally inserts the row, bumps `lastModified`, emits `message:sent`/`received`, and calls `onMessageToAgent(message)`. No existence check anywhere.
- `153419` — the immediate-wake hook `handleMessageToAgent` does `const agent = this.configStore.getCachedAgent(message.toId); if (!agent) { return; }` — for an unknown recipient it silently returns, so no wake fires.
- `145655` — delivery/read otherwise happens only when the recipient itself runs `fn_read_messages`, which calls `messageStore.getInbox(agentId, "agent", ...)` keyed by the caller's **own** id. A nonexistent agent never runs, never reads. No dead-letter or warning exists anywhere on this path.

## Reproduction

1. From any running agent, call `fn_send_message` with `to_id` set to an id not in the agents table (e.g. a role name like `CPO`, or a made-up id).
2. Observe: the tool returns `Message sent to <to_id> (ID: msg-...)`; a row is inserted (`toType=agent`, `read=0`); `getCachedAgent(<to_id>)` returns undefined so no wake fires; and no agent ever reads it.
3. The message stays `read=0` permanently, with no error surfaced to sender or operator.

## Suggested fix

In the send tool's `execute` (~`145289`–`145292`), before calling `messageStore.sendMessage` for agent-to-agent messages, resolve `params.to_id` against the agent config/store (e.g. `configStore.getCachedAgent` / agents lookup). If the recipient agent id does not exist, either return an ERROR result (symmetric with the existing empty-content / `reply_to` validations) or store-and-return a delivery WARNING so the sending agent knows the message was undeliverable. Alternatively/additionally validate in `MessageStore.sendMessage` (`dist/bin.js:33682`). Low-effort, self-contained; no schema change required.

## Version

0.60.0

title: [Bug] fn_delegate_task duplicate path drops the requested assignee but reports "Delegated to X"; no tool can reassign a task's owner by id
body:
# fn_delegate_task duplicate path drops the requested assignee but reports "Delegated to X"; no tool can reassign a task's owner by id

## Summary

When `fn_delegate_task` hits the deterministic duplicate guard, it returns the pre-existing task **unmodified** — the requested `assignedAgentId` (and `column`) are never applied — yet the tool still reports `Delegated to <name> (<id>): ... will be picked up by <name> on their next heartbeat cycle`. The existing task keeps whatever owner it already had (often none). And no agent-facing tool can reassign an existing task's owner by id, so the mis-delegation cannot be corrected.

## Expected vs Actual

- **Expected:** Either the existing task is (re)assigned to the requested agent, or the tool reports honestly that it linked to an existing task whose owner was **not** changed.
- **Actual:** On the duplicate path the assignment is silently discarded, but the success message unconditionally asserts "Delegated to X ... picked up by X." There is no tool to then set the task's owner by id.

## Root cause (exact locations, dist/bin.js)

- `146033` — `delegateTaskParams` accepts only `agent_id` + free-text `description` (no `task_id`), so an agent cannot target an existing task by id at all.
- `145189` — `fn_delegate_task` calls `createAgentTask({ description, dependencies, column: "todo", assignedAgentId: params.agent_id, ... })`, passing the intended owner in.
- `142924` — `createAgentTask` first runs `runDeterministicDuplicateGuard`.
- `35989` — the fingerprint is `sha256(normalizedTitle + "\n" + normalizedDescription)` (exact-match normalization), so a reworded description yields a different fingerprint and escapes the guard entirely (breeding a new card) rather than deduping.
- `36165` — on a hit the guard returns `{ action: "duplicate", existing: <raw matched task> }`; the matched task's owner is never mutated.
- `142934` — the duplicate branch `if (guard.action === "duplicate" && guard.existing) { return { task: guard.existing, wasDuplicate: true }; }` short-circuits **before** `store.createTask(createInput)` at `142962`, so the `assignedAgentId`/`column` carried in `createInput` are silently discarded.
- `145205` — the tool then unconditionally emits `Delegated to ${agent.name} (${agent.id}): ${wasDuplicate ? "Linked existing" : "Created"} ${task.id}... will be picked up by ${agent.name} on their next heartbeat cycle.` — the "Delegated to"/"picked up by X" framing prints even when `wasDuplicate=true` and no assignment occurred.

No remedy tool exists:
- `200493` — `fn_task_update` destructures only `{ step, status, dependencies, custom_fields }` and rejects a call with none of them; it has no `assignedAgentId` affordance.
- A grep for `fn_task_assign`/`fn_task_reassign`/`fn_reassign` returns zero hits. The only near-miss is `fn_delete_agent`'s bulk `reassign_to` param at `146087` — a bulk replacement during agent deletion, not a targeted per-task reassignment.

Secondary (latent) assignment-loss path, not required for the primary defect:
- `36189` — `reconcileDeterministicDuplicate`, after `store.createTask`, can find an older concurrent sibling and `moveTask` the freshly-created (correctly-owned) task to `archived`, returning the older sibling as canonical — again discarding the new `assignedAgentId`, even when the guard did not fire.

## Reproduction (code-level, definitive)

1. Call `fn_delegate_task(agent_id=A, description=D)`, creating task T.
2. Call `fn_delegate_task(agent_id=B, description=D)` with the same normalized description within the dedup window: the guard returns existing T, `createAgentTask` returns `{ task: T, wasDuplicate: true }` **without** applying `assignedAgentId=B`. The tool prints "Delegated to B ... picked up by B," but T's owner is unchanged.
3. Attempt to fix it: no `fn_task_*` tool can set T's `assignedAgentId` to B.

(Reworded re-delegation — `"Fix: " + D` — produces a different fingerprint, escapes the guard, and breeds a new card.)

## Scope / honesty note

The code mechanism above (duplicate short-circuit before `createTask`; misleading unconditional success message; `fn_task_update` has no owner field; no reassign-by-id tool) is confirmed at high confidence and is config-independent — a default-config user hits it. The broader "breeding"/reconcile-archive narrative should be treated as **latent/secondary**: in our data those effects were not demonstrated to originate from this tool (the reconcile-archive path had not fired, and reworded task families were attributable to a different subsystem). File the confirmed core; present the rest as latent risk.

## Suggested fix

Preferred, in `createAgentTask` (`142934`–`142936`): when `guard.action === "duplicate"`, apply the caller's assignment/column to the existing task before returning — e.g. if `input.assignedAgentId` differs from `guard.existing.assignedAgentId`, `await store.updateTask(guard.existing.id, { assignedAgentId: input.assignedAgentId, column: input.column })` and return the refreshed task; carry assignment onto the canonical in `reconcileDeterministicDuplicate` (`36185`–`36211`) too.

And/or add a dedicated `fn_task_assign(task_id, agent_id)` agent tool (mirroring the delegate bind checks around `145162`–`145186`), since none exists.

At minimum, make the message truthful: when `wasDuplicate` is true, `fn_delegate_task` (`145202`–`145206`) should say it linked to an existing task whose owner was NOT changed (reporting `task.assignedAgentId`) rather than "Delegated to X ... will be picked up by X."

## Version

0.60.0

title: [Bug] Feature validator inspects the working checkout's ambient branch instead of the integration branch where deliverables merged; its stale-workspace guard is dead code (fail-open), turning branch divergence into hard FAILs
body:
# Feature validator inspects the working checkout's ambient branch instead of the integration branch where deliverables merged; its stale-workspace guard is dead code (fail-open), turning branch divergence into hard FAILs

## Summary

The read-only feature validator ("judge") runs in the mission-loop project root (`rootDir`) and inspects **whatever branch that checkout currently has at HEAD**. It never checks out the task's merge-target / integration branch, and never uses the branch-group integration worktree — even though the merger deliberately integrates each task in a **separate** integration root/worktree on the integration branch. When the working checkout sits on one branch (e.g. the repo default) while deliverables merged onto a divergent integration branch, the judge reads the wrong tree, reports the deliverables "absent," and records a hard FAIL — a false negative that feeds a re-spec loop.

The one safety net designed to catch exactly this — a stale-workspace guard that would downgrade such a FAIL to a deferred "inconclusive" — is **dead code**: it resolves the integration SHA from task fields that do not exist, always gets `undefined`, and fails open.

### Scope / honesty note
A divergence between the working checkout's branch and the integration branch is an operator-side **trigger** (in our case a repo-default vs integration-branch split, since reconciled). This report is NOT about that divergence. It is about two config-independent Fusion code defects that convert a recoverable "inconclusive" into hard FAILs plus a re-spec loop: (1) the validator is never pinned to the integration branch it merged into, and (2) the stale-workspace guard is dead.

## Expected vs Actual

- **Expected:** The validator inspects the branch/commit where the work actually landed (the task's merge-target / integration branch, or the branch-group integration worktree). If the merged code is not present in the inspected tree, the run is deferred as "inconclusive," never a hard FAIL.
- **Actual:** The validator inspects the ambient HEAD of the shared checkout; when that differs from the integration branch it reports deliverables absent and records a hard FAIL, driving a fix/re-spec loop.

## Root cause (exact locations, dist/bin.js)

- `207113` — `cwd: this.rootDir`: the judge session is created against the mission-loop project root. The surrounding `runValidation` body performs **no** checkout and no pinning to the task's merge-target/integration branch.
- `169933` — `rootDir = integrationRoot.rootDir` (inside the merge function): the merger operates in a **separate** integration root, checking out the merge-target branch there (`170499`–`170503`) before merging. So merged deliverables land on the integration branch in a worktree distinct from the validator's `rootDir` — an asymmetry the validator does not mirror.
- `134955`–`134967` — `resolveIntegrationBranch` resolves `settings.integrationBranch/baseBranch` → `origin/HEAD` → fallback constant. With no project override the integration branch is decided independently of the branch the primary checkout sits on. (The precise derivation of a given branch-group's recorded branch name at creation time is not fully established from present state; the load-bearing point is only that resolution is independent of the working checkout's HEAD.)

Dead stale-workspace guard:
- `207065`–`207077` — `isValidationWorkspaceStale`: `const integrationSha = await this.resolveIntegrationSha(feature); if (!integrationSha) return false;` — fails **open** (trusts the fail) when the SHA is unresolvable.
- `207293` — `resolveIntegrationSha` returns `candidate?.integrationSha ?? candidate?.baseCommit ?? void 0`.
- `54958` — the hydrated task's canonical field is `baseCommitSha` (not `integrationSha`, not `baseCommit`). Neither property read at `207293` exists on the task, so `resolveIntegrationSha` **always** returns `undefined` → the guard always returns false → the fail path (`handleValidationFail`) always runs instead of `handleValidationInconclusive`. Note also that even if it read `baseCommitSha`, that is the **base** commit, not the integration merge tip; the actual merge tip lives in `mergeDetails.commitSha`.
- Internal contradiction worth noting: `resolveIntegrationSha`'s own docstring (~`207280`–`207286`) states an unresolved SHA should cause "inconclusive (fail-closed)," but the code at `207067` returns false (fail-**open** → hard fail). The doc/code mismatch corroborates that the fail-open behavior is a bug.

Bookkeeping gap (separate but related):
- `207011` — `startValidatorRun(feature.id, "task_completion")` is called without the `taskId` argument, so validator-run records lose their task/branch linkage (`taskId` null on every run).

## Reproduction

1. On a repo where the working checkout (`rootDir`) sits on one branch while the mission branch-group integration branch resolves to a different, divergent branch (no `settings.integrationBranch` override).
2. Run a mission feature whose task merges to the integration branch while `rootDir` stays on the other branch.
3. Move the linked task to done to trigger feature validation. The judge (cwd = `rootDir`) reports the deliverable "absent"; because `resolveIntegrationSha` yields `undefined`, `isValidationWorkspaceStale` returns false and the run is recorded as a hard FAIL rather than a deferred inconclusive, driving a fix/re-spec loop.
4. Observationally confirmed: once the working checkout reflected the branch holding the deliverables, the same feature validated as PASS.

## Suggested fix

In `runValidation` (~`207085`–`207152`) and `isValidationWorkspaceStale`/`resolveIntegrationSha` (~`207065`–`207297`):
1. Pin the validator's inspection to the branch/commit where the work landed — check out (or `cwd`) the task's merge-target/integration branch (`resolveIntegrationBranch` result) or the branch-group integration worktree, rather than trusting `rootDir`'s ambient HEAD.
2. Make `resolveIntegrationSha` actually resolve the integration tip: read `task.baseCommitSha` and, critically, the merged commit from `mergeDetails.commitSha` (it currently reads non-existent `task.integrationSha`/`task.baseCommit` and always returns undefined), and align the guard with its documented fail-closed intent so a wrong-branch fail downgrades to "inconclusive."
3. Pass `taskId` into `startValidatorRun` (`207011`) so runs retain task linkage.

## Version

0.60.0

title: [Bug] Shared auth.json lock: synchronous reload/persist path retries only ~180ms with an event-loop-blocking busy-wait, terminating runs under concurrency
body:
# Shared auth.json lock: synchronous reload/persist path retries only ~180ms with an event-loop-blocking busy-wait, terminating runs under concurrency

## Summary

Every Claude-Code session the engine spawns reads and writes a single shared credential file at `~/.fusion/agent/auth.json`, serialized by `proper-lockfile` on `auth.json.lock`. The **synchronous** lock path used by `reload()` and `persistProviderChange()` retries only ~180ms (10 attempts × 20ms) using a synchronous, event-loop-blocking busy-wait, then throws `ELOCKED ("Lock file is already being held")` and terminates the run. The sibling **asynchronous** path guards the *same* lock with proper exponential backoff (~10s). Under elevated concurrency (or a synchronized timer-triggered burst of runs), the ~180ms sync budget is exhausted and runs die.

Note: an earlier hypothesis framed this as "fails fast with no retry/backoff." That is inaccurate — there **is** a retry; it is just grossly undersized versus the async path and it blocks the event loop.

## Expected vs Actual

- **Expected:** Concurrent credential reload/persist should wait/back off on lock contention the same way the async token-refresh path does (up to ~10s exponential backoff), so transient contention does not kill a run.
- **Actual:** The sync path spins ~180ms (busy-wait, blocking the worker's event loop) and then throws `ELOCKED`, terminating the run.

## Root cause (exact locations)

Credential path and wiring (bundle):
- `dist/bin.js:96050` — `getFusionAuthPath()` → `~/.fusion/agent/auth.json` (single fixed shared path, no per-run component).
- `dist/bin.js:96238` — `AuthStorage.create(getFusionAuthPath())` wires the shared file; every spawned session contends on it.
- `dist/bin.js:228548` — origin of the `ELOCKED` "Lock file is already being held" string (bundled `proper-lockfile`, default `retries:0`).
- `dist/bin.js:228882` — the only `retries:0` `proper-lockfile.lock` call guards the single-instance **engine** lock (`engine.lock`), NOT auth; it is a decoy, not the terminating path.

The defect lives in the shipped dependency `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent` (`dist/core/auth-storage.js`), which Fusion configures and ships:
- `acquireLockSyncWithRetry` (lines ~34–57): `maxAttempts=10`, `delayMs=20`, calls `lockfile.lockSync(path,{realpath:false})` (no `retries` option → `proper-lockfile` default `retries:0`, fails immediately on `ELOCKED`) inside a loop whose delay is a **synchronous busy-wait** `while (Date.now()-start < delayMs) {}`. Total budget ≈ 9 × 20ms ≈ 180ms, and each spin blocks the event loop. Throws `ELOCKED` when exhausted.
- `withLockAsync` (lines ~78–125): guards the **same** lock with `lock(this.authPath, { retries:{ retries:10, factor:2, minTimeout:100, maxTimeout:10000, randomize:true }, stale:30000 })` — proper exponential backoff to ~10s.
- `reload()` (line ~197) and `persistProviderChange()` (line ~220) both route through the **sync** path; only `refreshOAuthTokenWithLock` uses the async path.
- `persistProviderChange` rethrow (line ~214): `Cannot update auth storage because it could not be loaded: <msg>` — the second observed terminating signature, produced when a sync `reload()` lost the race first.
- `getApiKey` catch (lines ~408–419): the async path's `ELOCKED` is **swallowed** (reload + return undefined). This positively rules the async path out — both run-terminating stderr signatures (`Error: Lock file is already being held` and `Error: Cannot update auth storage because it could not be loaded: ...`) originate exclusively from the undersized **sync** path.

## Reproduction

1. Point ~10–12 concurrent sessions at the same `~/.fusion/agent/auth.json` holding an OAuth credential near expiry (so each triggers a persist on refresh).
2. Fire them simultaneously (a synchronized timer burst reproduces it well).
3. Multiple sessions enter `acquireLockSyncWithRetry`; because `lockSync` uses `retries:0` and the outer loop grants only ~180ms (busy-waiting, blocking each worker's loop), any contention lasting >~180ms throws `ELOCKED` and terminates the run — either the raw string or the `Cannot update auth storage ...` variant.

Observed impact under elevated concurrency: a cluster of ~6 timer-triggered lock-loss terminations within ~5.5 seconds, plus a broader tail of such terminations across agents.

## Severity note (concurrency-dependent)

Defaults are `globalMaxConcurrent=4` (`dist/bin.js:1365`) and per-project `maxConcurrent=2` (`dist/bin.js:1363`). A default-config deployment with short lock holds will usually stay within the ~180ms budget and NOT see terminations. The terminations were observed at 3–6× elevated concurrency plus synchronized bursts. **However**, the underlying defect is config-independent: the same shared, hardcoded lock is retried ~180ms via an event-loop-blocking busy-wait on the sync reload/persist path while the async caller of the same lock gets ~10s exponential backoff. That internal inconsistency is worth fixing regardless of who trips it.

## Suggested fix

In `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent` `acquireLockSyncWithRetry`:
- Raise the retry budget and use real exponential backoff to match `withLockAsync` (`retries:10, factor:2, minTimeout:100, maxTimeout:10000`).
- Remove the synchronous busy-wait that blocks the event loop — ideally route `reload()`/`persistProviderChange()` through the existing async `withLockAsync` path.

Complementary Fusion-side mitigation to reduce contention: don't funnel every spawned session through one shared file — serialize auth writes in-process, or give runs isolated/read-mostly credential copies (`getFusionAuthPath` at `dist/bin.js:96050` / `AuthStorage.create` at `dist/bin.js:96238`). Filed against Fusion because Fusion ships this dependency and configures the shared path.

## Version

0.60.0

title: Problème : la bordure « agent actif » des cartes ne reflète pas l'activité réelle
body:
Symptôme
Sur le board, certaines cartes de tâches perdent leur bordure clignotante (le contour animé qui signale qu'un agent travaille activement dessus), alors même qu'un agent est bel et bien en train de s'exécuter dessus — les logs de la tâche affichent des résultats « à l'instant ». Inversement, la bordure peut s'éteindre en plein milieu d'une phase active, ou rester allumée sur des états où l'agent n'est plus réellement actif. Le signal visuel de la carte (bordure) et le signal de progression (badge / logs) se contredisent pour une même tâche au même instant.

Contexte observable
Une tâche en colonne in-review, en pleine revue par le reviewer/merger (logs frais), peut s'afficher sans bordure.
Une tâche en colonne planning/triage, pendant une Plan Review active (badge « Reviewing » affiché, logs frais), peut elle aussi s'afficher sans bordure.
Le badge de statut et la bordure ne sont pas toujours d'accord : le badge peut indiquer une activité en cours pendant que la bordure reste éteinte.
Ce qui semble en jeu
La bordure clignotante semble pilotée par le statut (task.status) de la tâche, via une liste fixe de statuts considérés comme « actifs ». Or ce statut ne décrit pas toujours fidèlement l'activité réelle de l'agent :

Il existe des phases où un agent tourne activement alors que le statut est null ou dans un état « parqué » (ex. retry après outage reviewer).
Il existe des statuts de sous-phase active (ex. reviewing, merging-pr) qui ne semblent pas reconnus comme actifs par la bordure, alors qu'ils le sont pour le comptage de concurrence côté engine.
Le badge de progression, lui, semble se baser sur un signal différent (les étapes / steps du workflow), ce qui explique la contradiction entre badge et bordure.
Hypothèse de l'utilisateur (à confirmer par l'analyse du code)
Il y aurait plusieurs notions indépendantes de « agent actif » qui ne s'accordent pas : celle qui pilote la bordure (basée sur task.status + une liste de statuts), celle qui pilote le badge (basée sur les steps), et celle utilisée par l'engine pour le comptage des slots (column-aware). Ces notions auraient dérivé les unes des autres, de sorte qu'aucune ne capture à elle seule la vérité terrain « un agent est en train de travailler sur cette tâche maintenant ».

Ce que j'attends
Identifier la cause exacte dans le code (pourquoi la bordure s'éteint alors que l'agent tourne), puis proposer une correction pour que la bordure reflète fidèlement l'activité réelle de l'agent, de façon cohérente avec le badge et le compteur de concurrence — sans maintenir de listes parallèles qui risquent de dériver à nouveau.

title: `taskTokenBudget` soft/hard caps are silently ignored — the FN-4396 enforcer module has zero call sites
body:
## Summary

`taskTokenBudget` (global and project level, including `perSize`) is accepted, validated, persisted, and documented — but never enforced. The enforcement module written for FN-4396 (#257) exists in the repo and passes its unit test, yet nothing in the engine ever calls it, so bundlers exclude it from the published package entirely. The practical result: runaway tasks blow straight through their hard caps with no alert, no pause, and no notification.

## Environment

- `@runfusion/fusion` **0.60.0** (npm, latest at time of writing), Windows 11, server started via `fn dashboard`
- Findings also verified against `main` @ `22fde62` (2026-07-16)

## Expected behavior (per `docs/settings-reference.md`)

> Soft cap sends a one-time alert per task; hard cap pauses the task with `pausedReason: "token_budget_exceeded"`.

Resolution precedence: task override → project `perSize` → project base → global `perSize` → global base.

## Actual behavior

Nothing ever fires. From a real deployment (project settings `taskTokenBudget: {"soft":6000000,"hard":15000000,"perSize":{"M":{"soft":6000000,"hard":15000000},...}}`):

- A **size-M task reached 55.7M `totalTokens`** — 3.7× its 15M hard cap — and was never alerted, paused, or notified.
- `tokenBudgetSoftAlertedAt` / `tokenBudgetHardAlertedAt` are `NULL` on **every** task in the database.
- No `"token-budget"` notification has ever been dispatched.

## Root cause

The FN-4396 merge (9f24f1bb05ad013db3a77548b139d32586f5f607, closed #257 as completed) added `packages/engine/src/token-budget-enforcer.ts` (+88 lines — full soft/hard/perSize resolution and pause logic, looks correct) plus a unit test. But the integration step never landed:

1. **Zero call sites.** Across the entire current source tree, `enforceTaskTokenBudget` / `token-budget-enforcer` are referenced only by the module itself, its own unit test, and `docs/test-value-audit.json`. The module has exactly one commit in its history (the FN-4396 merge, 2026-05-14) and has never been touched since.

2. **Dead code is excluded from the published package.** Since nothing imports the module, the published `dist/bin.js` (checked 0.60.0) contains no trace of it: nothing reads `taskTokenBudget.soft`/`.hard`, the string `perSize` does not appear anywhere in any shipped bundle, nothing ever writes `tokenBudgetSoftAlertedAt`/`tokenBudgetHardAlertedAt`, and nothing dispatches the `"token-budget"` notification event.

3. **The only engine-side trace is an exclusion rule for a state nothing can reach:** self-healing excludes `pausedReason: "token_budget_exceeded"` from its scope-decay sweep — but no code ever sets that reason.

4. **Why CI stayed green:** the unit test (`token-budget-enforcer.test.ts`) mocks the store with `vi.fn()` and calls the enforcer directly, so it verifies the module in isolation and cannot detect that the engine never invokes it.

5. **Surrounding plumbing all exists**, which makes the gap invisible to users: the settings schema accepts and persists the key, the DB migration creates the alert columns, `"token-budget"` is a registered notification event (`packages/core/src/settings-schema.ts:138`, `packages/engine/src/notifier.ts:38`, and even a workflow-step notification option in `packages/core/src/types/workflow-steps.ts:105`), and `docs/settings-reference.md` documents the feature as working.

Affected releases: every version from **v0.30.0 (2026-05-15, first release after the merge) through v0.60.0 (current)**.

## Reproduction

1. Set project settings: `taskTokenBudget: {"soft": 1000, "hard": 2000}`.
2. Run any task that consumes more than 2,000 tokens (i.e., any task).
3. Observe: the task keeps running past both caps; `tokenBudgetSoftAlertedAt`/`tokenBudgetHardAlertedAt` stay `NULL`; no `"token-budget"` notification is dispatched.

## Suggested fix

Invoke `enforceTaskTokenBudget` wherever task token usage is persisted, i.e. right after each `store.updateTask(taskId, { tokenUsage })`:

- `packages/engine/src/session-token-usage.ts:159` (inside `accumulateSessionTokenUsage` — the shared helper),
- `packages/engine/src/executor.ts:4253`,
- the step-accumulation sites in `executor.ts` (~10445, 10494, 10813, 10819),

and wire its `notify` callback to dispatch the already-registered `"token-budget"` notification event.

## One design note worth deciding before wiring it up

The enforcer compares budgets against `tokenUsage.totalTokens`, which includes `cachedTokens` + `cacheWriteTokens`. In our runaway example, **96% of the 55.7M total (53.6M) was cache reads**; genuinely new tokens were ~2.1M. Once enforcement goes live, budgets calibrated in "real work" terms will trip almost immediately on cache-heavy sessions. It's worth either documenting explicitly that budgets are cache-inclusive, or resolving budgets against a non-cache basis (e.g. `input + output + cacheWrite`) — otherwise every existing `taskTokenBudget` config in the wild will behave very differently from what its author intended.

title: Le tchat d'une tache dans la modale de détail des taches doit être "pro-actif". c'est à dire qu'au lieu de servir que d'entrée pour l'utilisateur et servir des réponses, celui-ci doit également et avant tout servir de compte-rendu en temps réel.
body:
Le tchat d'une tache dans la modale de détail des taches doit être "pro-actif". c'est à dire qu'au lieu de servir que d'entrée pour l'utilisateur et servir des réponses, celui-ci doit également et avant tout servir de compte-rendu en temps réel.
Concretement, à chaque step accompli dans la tache, l'agent du tchat doit y écrire ce qu'il s'est passé.

Par exemple : 
"Le plan a été correctement rédigé et vérifié"
"l'étape X vient de se terminer, la fonctionnalité X a été implémentée"

Mais ça doit aussi dire quand quelque chose se passe mal, quand une étape n'est pas passée avec succès, quand un rollback de step a eu lieu en expliquant ce qu'il s'est passé, c'est à dire la raison pour laquelle il y a eu une erreur.

L'interet est que ce chat soit pro actif, affiche des informations utiles pour l'utilisateur, des informations sur lesquelles l'utilisateur pourra rebondir.

title: triageDefaultWorkflowId enum rejects custom workflow IDs — project default workflow setting is ignored at triage time
body:
## Bug: triageDefaultWorkflowId cannot reference custom workflows

**Version:** Fusion v0.6 (still occurring)

## What happens

The per-workflow setting `triageDefaultWorkflowId` is declared as a 2-option enum (`builtin:coding` / `builtin:quick-fix`). It cannot be set to any custom workflow ID such as `WF-005`.

Meanwhile, `config.settings` does support `defaultWorkflowId: "WF-005"` and the UI reflects it as the project default. However, the triage engine appears to read the per-workflow `triageDefaultWorkflowId` setting (not the config default) when routing new Dashboard/API tasks. Because the enum cannot reference `WF-005`, new tasks are stamped with `builtin:coding` regardless of the configured project default.

## Observed behavior

- Project `config.settings` has `defaultWorkflowId: "WF-005"` ("Coding v2")
- `fn_workflow_settings get` for `WF-005` returns `triageDefaultWorkflowId: "builtin:coding"` and rejects any attempt to set it to `WF-005` with `enum-violation`
- New tasks created via Dashboard/API consistently land on `builtin:coding`, not `WF-005`
- Operators see the UI default as Coding v2 but actual task routing uses the built-in Coding workflow

## Expected behavior

`triageDefaultWorkflowId` should accept any valid workflow ID (including custom workflows), or the triage engine should fall through to `config.settings.defaultWorkflowId` when the per-workflow value is the schema default rather than an explicit operator choice.

## Impact

- Project-level workflow defaults are silently ignored
- Custom workflows like `WF-005` (Coding v2) cannot become the effective intake workflow through configuration alone
- Operators must manually re-route every task with `fn_workflow_select` after creation

## Workaround

Manually call `fn_workflow_select(workflow_id="WF-005")` on every new task after creation. This is not viable at scale.

## Reproduction

1. Create a custom workflow (e.g. `WF-005`) and set `config.settings.defaultWorkflowId` to it
2. Create a new task via Dashboard or API
3. Observe the task is assigned `builtin:coding`, not `WF-005`
4. Attempt `fn_workflow_settings set workflow_id=WF-005 values={triageDefaultWorkflowId: WF-005}` — observe `enum-violation` rejection

title: Tasks cycling back to planning/triage after entering in-progress (provider failure causes state regression)
body:
## Bug: Tasks regress to planning/triage after entering execution

**Version:** Fusion v0.6 (still occurring)

## What happens

Tasks that successfully enter `in-progress` (or `todo`) are being moved back to `planning`/`triage` when the triage/planning LLM provider returns a transient error (429 rate-limit, 403, provider outage). The engine treats the planning-step failure as grounds to undo task execution state and return the card to the planning column.

## Observed behavior

- NEXT-1363 entered planning, hit `Triage failed: unable to select a usable model after 2 attempts` (antigravity/gemini-3.5-flash-low, 429 + 403), and was queued for retry with a 114s cooldown.
- Multiple prior tasks in this session exhibited the same pattern: entered `in-progress`, took execution steps, then were ejected back to `planning` after a provider-side triage/planning retry.
- Only 1 task has reached `done` in the current session despite multiple tasks being actively worked.

## Expected behavior

A transient provider failure during triage/planning should **retry in place** (or queue a retry) without regressing the task's column/state. Once a task has left planning and entered execution, a planning-step retry must not undo that progress.

## Root cause hypothesis

The workflow graph's `plan` node has an edge `plan → end (failure)` and/or the engine re-evaluates the planning step on a task that has already advanced, treating the new planning failure as a terminal condition and resetting the card. The `stuck_specification_retries` detector catches the symptom but does not prevent the state regression.

## Impact

- Significant loss of execution progress on every provider hiccup
- Board churn: tasks bounce between columns, confusing operators
- Executor work is discarded or orphaned when a task regresses after commits have been made in its worktree

## Related

- Internal tracking: NEXT-1225 (stuck_specification_retries investigation)
- Observed in project: NextGenEHS (Automata-intelligentsia/NextGenEHS)

title: Feature: Skills-view toggle on plugin skills with custom skillFiles paths persists a dead entry — state snaps back and never reaches sessions
body:
**Fusion version:** 0.60.0 (installed `@runfusion/fusion@0.60.0`, evidence from `dist/bin.js`)
**Area:** Skills view / per-project plugin-skill enablement
**Severity:** High — per-project skill toggles are silently non-functional for any plugin skill whose `skillFiles[0]` is not `skills/<name>/SKILL.md`
**Related:** #2016 (session merge toggle honoring, fixed), #2018 (`skillFiles` honored for body path, fixed) — this bug is the remaining key-schema mismatch between the write path and the two read paths.

## Summary

Since #2018, a plugin skill's body may live at a custom path declared in `skillFiles[0]`
(e.g. a category layout `skills/data/ef-core/SKILL.md`). The Skills view lists such skills
correctly, but **toggling them is a no-op**:

- flip a toggle → navigate away → return: the toggle has reverted;
- the change never reaches session assembly either (`<available_skills>` unchanged).

Toggles written **before** a skill moved to a custom path (stored under the name-derived
key) keep working — masking the bug in upgraded projects and breaking only *fresh* toggles.

## Root cause — write and read paths use different key schemas

All in `dist/bin.js` (0.60.0):

- **Write path:** `toggleExecutionSkill` derives the entry from the skill's **resolved body
  path** and persists `+/-<skillFiles-relative-path>` (e.g. `-data/ef-core/SKILL.md`) under
  the project's `packages[{source:'plugin:<id>'}].skills`.
- **Read path (display AND session merge):** `resolvePluginSkillEnabled` →
  `computeSkillId('plugin:' + pluginId, 'skills/' + name + '/SKILL.md')` →
  `getSkillSettingState` — i.e. lookup is keyed by the **NAME-derived path**
  (`<name>/SKILL.md`), matched by exact normalized-path equality or recomputed entryId.

`data/ef-core/SKILL.md` never equals `ef-core/SKILL.md`, so the persisted toggle entry is
dead: displayed state falls back to the static registration default, and session merge
ignores the toggle likewise.

## Reproduction

1. Register a plugin whose skill declares `skillFiles: ["skills/<category>/<name>/SKILL.md"]`
   (any path other than `skills/<name>/SKILL.md`).
2. In a project's Skills view, toggle that skill (either direction).
3. Navigate away and back: the toggle has reverted.
4. Project settings show the persisted dead entry (`+/-<category>/<name>/SKILL.md`); start a
   session: `<available_skills>` does not reflect the toggle.

## Expected

Write and read paths key toggle state identically — either both by the resolved
`skillFiles` body path or both by the name-derived path — so a Skills-view flip is
displayed persistently and honored by session merge.

title: compound-engineering: all 12 SKILL.md files missing from npm tarball, skills silently never load
body:
## Summary

The compound-engineering plugin declares 12 skills (`ce-plan`, `ce-work`, `ce-code-review`, `ce-strategy`, `ce-debug`, `ce-brainstorm`, `ce-commit`, `ce-commit-push-pr`, `ce-compound`, `ce-doc-review`, `ce-ideate`, `ce-resolve-pr-feedback`), but **none of their `SKILL.md` files ship in the npm tarball**. The skills root resolves to a directory that does not exist, so the CE skills silently never load and sessions fall back to generic role skills.

Silent failure — no error, no warning. The plugin enables and reports healthy.

## Detail

The skills exist in the repo at `plugins/fusion-plugin-compound-engineering/src/skills/` (verified at tag `v0.60.0` — 12 dirs, 121 files), but the published package contains only:

```
dist/plugins/fusion-plugin-compound-engineering/
  bundled.js
  manifest.json
  package.json
```

No `src/skills/`, no `skills/`. Confirmed with `npm pack @runfusion/fusion@0.60.0 --dry-run`:

```bash
npm pack @runfusion/fusion@0.60.0
tar tzf runfusion-fusion-0.60.0.tgz | grep -c 'skills/'   # 0
```

So `resolveBundledSkillsRoot()` has nothing to point at.

This is distinct from #2017 / #2018 (`PluginSkillContribution.skillFiles` never read by the host). That fix wires up *delivery* of a `SKILL.md` that exists on disk — it does not help when the files were never published in the first place.

## Impact

Anyone installing from npm and enabling compound-engineering gets the workflow graph (`plan` -> `plan-review` -> `execute` -> `code-review`) but **not** the skill bodies that are supposed to drive those steps. The degradation is invisible.

## Workaround

Fetch the skills from the repo at the tag matching the installed version and point the plugin at them:

```bash
D=$(npm root -g)/@runfusion/fusion
gh api repos/Runfusion/Fusion/tarball/v0.60.0 > /tmp/f.tar.gz
root=$(tar tzf /tmp/f.tar.gz | head -1 | cut -d/ -f1)
mkdir -p $D/dist/plugins/src/skills
tar xzf /tmp/f.tar.gz -C $D/dist/plugins/src/skills --strip-components=5 \
  "$root/plugins/fusion-plugin-compound-engineering/src/skills"

export FUSION_CE_SKILLS_DIR="$D/dist/plugins/src/skills"
```

The env var is required — with the files merely on disk the skills stay undiscovered. After this, the plugin emits `compound-engineering:skills-installed` for all 12 and `ce-plan` resolves.

## Fix

Include the skills directory in the CLI package's published `files`, alongside the plugin's `bundled.js`.

## Environment

- `@runfusion/fusion` 0.60.0 (also 0.59.0)
- Node v24.14.1, macOS 15 (arm64)

title: reports / cli-printing-press / whatsapp-chat ship unbundled .ts: ERR_UNSUPPORTED_NODE_MODULES_TYPE_STRIPPING, plugins cannot load
body:
## Summary

Three plugins ship as raw TypeScript source with **no prebuilt `bundled.js`**, unlike the other 11. Node refuses to strip TS types under `node_modules`, so they cannot load at all:

```
fnlvl=error [plugin-loader] Failed to load plugin fusion-plugin-reports:
Error [ERR_UNSUPPORTED_NODE_MODULES_TYPE_STRIPPING]: Stripping types is currently
unsupported for files under node_modules, for
".../dist/plugins/fusion-plugin-reports/src/.index.reload-13.ts"
```

Affected: `fusion-plugin-reports`, `fusion-plugin-cli-printing-press`, `fusion-plugin-whatsapp-chat`.

`fusion-plugin-reports` is enabled by default, so this surfaces immediately in the UI as a red **ERROR** badge on the Reports plugin card.

## Detail

Confirmed against the published tarball (`npm pack @runfusion/fusion@0.60.0`):

- 11 plugins ship `bundled.js` + `manifest.json` + `package.json` — these load fine.
- These 3 ship `manifest.json` + `package.json` + the entire `src/` tree (including `__tests__/*.test.ts`) and **no** `bundled.js`.

Their `package.json` `exports` point straight at TypeScript:

```json
"exports": {
  ".": { "types": "./src/index.ts", "import": "./src/index.ts" }
}
```

Node's type-stripping is explicitly disabled for anything under `node_modules`, which is exactly where a global install puts them, so the entry can never be loaded. The `.index.reload-N.ts` file in the error is the hot-reload copy the loader writes next to the source.

This looks like the same class of packaging inconsistency as #2059 — `docs/PLUGIN_AUTHORING.md` describes the self-contained-bundle contract, but nothing enforces it for these three.

## Reproduce

```bash
npm i -g @runfusion/fusion@0.60.0
D=$(npm root -g)/@runfusion/fusion
ls $D/dist/plugins/fusion-plugin-reports/          # manifest.json package.json src  -- no bundled.js
ls $D/dist/plugins/fusion-plugin-roadmap/          # bundled.js manifest.json package.json
fusion dashboard                                   # Reports card shows ERROR
```

## Workaround

Prebuild the bundle the same way the working plugins are built, and point `exports` at it:

```bash
cd $D/dist/plugins/fusion-plugin-reports
npx esbuild@0.25.0 src/index.ts --bundle --format=esm --platform=node --target=node24 \
  --outfile=bundled.js \
  --alias:@fusion/plugin-sdk=$D/dist/plugin-sdk/index.js \
  --alias:@fusion/core=$D/dist/plugin-sdk/index.js \
  --external:react --external:react-dom --external:lucide-react
```

Note the install record in `~/.fusion/fusion-central.db` (`plugin_installs.path`) pins the absolute entry path and must also be repointed at `bundled.js` — editing `exports` alone has no effect, since the loader uses the recorded path.

The proper fix is to bundle these three at publish time like the other 11.

## Environment

- `@runfusion/fusion` 0.60.0
- Node v24.14.1, macOS 15 (arm64)

title: pi-claude-cli extension fails to load: provider missing from model registry (pi-ai alias prefix-match + undeclared @agentclientprotocol/sdk)
body:
## Summary

The `pi-claude-cli` extension never loads, so the `pi-claude-cli` model provider is **absent from the model registry entirely**. Any agent configured with `pi-claude-cli/<model>` silently resolves to nothing.

Present in both 0.59.0 and 0.60.0. Reproduced in a clean `node:24.14.1-slim` container, so this is not a local-install artifact.

```
[extensions] Failed to load .../dist/pi-claude-cli/index.ts: Failed to load extension:
Cannot find module '.../@earendil-works/pi-ai/dist/compat.js/providers/all'
```

Note the impossible path: `dist/compat.js/providers/all` — a subpath appended onto a resolved *file*.

## Root cause 1 — alias map prefix-match

`@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/extensions/loader.js` aliases the pi-ai package **root** to the compat entrypoint:

```js
const piAiCompatEntry = resolveWorkspaceOrImport("ai/dist/compat.js", "@earendil-works/pi-ai/compat");
_aliases = {
    "@earendil-works/pi-ai": piAiCompatEntry,          // -> .../pi-ai/dist/compat.js
    "@earendil-works/pi-ai/compat": piAiCompatEntry,
    "@earendil-works/pi-ai/oauth": piAiOauthEntry,
    // no entry for ./providers/*
};
```

jiti prefix-matches alias keys. `dist/pi-claude-cli/index.ts` imports:

```ts
import { getBuiltinModels } from "@earendil-works/pi-ai/providers/all";
```

That specifier prefix-matches the bare `"@earendil-works/pi-ai"` key, gets rewritten to `.../dist/compat.js`, and the leftover `/providers/all` is concatenated — producing `dist/compat.js/providers/all`.

Aliases exist for `/compat` and `/oauth`, but not for `/providers/*`, which is the only pi-ai subpath `pi-claude-cli` imports.

Standard Node resolution can't rescue it either: `pi-ai@0.80.6` declares that subpath with **only an `import` condition, no `require`**:

```json
"./providers/*": { "types": "./dist/providers/*.d.ts", "import": "./dist/providers/*.js" }
```

so a CJS `require.resolve` fails with `ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED` (ESM `import` of the same specifier works fine).

## Root cause 2 — extension dependency never installed

After fixing the alias, loading fails again:

```
Cannot find module '@agentclientprotocol/sdk'
```

`dist/pi-claude-cli/package.json` declares `{"@agentclientprotocol/sdk": "0.24.0"}`, but the root `@runfusion/fusion` package.json does not, so npm never installs it. It is absent from `node_modules` in a fresh global install.

## Reproduce

```bash
npm i -g @runfusion/fusion@0.60.0
D=$(npm root -g)/@runfusion/fusion
cat > $D/dist/probe.mjs <<'JS'
import { createRequire } from "module";
const req = createRequire(import.meta.url);
try { req.resolve("@earendil-works/pi-ai/providers/all"); console.log("CJS: OK"); }
catch (e) { console.log("CJS:", e.code); }            // ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED
const m = await import("@earendil-works/pi-ai/providers/all");
console.log("ESM: OK", typeof m.getBuiltinModels);    // OK function
JS
node $D/dist/probe.mjs
ls $D/node_modules/@agentclientprotocol   # ENOENT
```

Then configure any agent with `modelProvider: "pi-claude-cli"` and boot the dashboard — the extension fails and `GET /api/models` returns only `anthropic`, `openrouter`, `cursor-cli`.

## Fix that works locally

1. Resolve and alias the subpath explicitly in `loader.js`:

```js
const piAiProvidersAllEntry = resolveWorkspaceOrImport("ai/dist/providers/all.js", "@earendil-works/pi-ai/providers/all");
// ...
"@earendil-works/pi-ai/providers/all": piAiProvidersAllEntry,
"@mariozechner/pi-ai/providers/all": piAiProvidersAllEntry,
```

2. Declare `@agentclientprotocol/sdk` as a dependency of the CLI package (or ship extension deps).

With both applied, the extension loads and `pi-claude-cli` registers 14 models (`claude-sonnet-5`, `claude-opus-4-8`, ...).

A general fix might be to make the alias map's root entry exact-match rather than prefix-match, so unaliased subpaths fall through to normal exports resolution instead of being silently mangled.

## Environment

- `@runfusion/fusion` 0.60.0 (also 0.59.0)
- Node v24.14.1, macOS 15 (arm64); reproduced in `node:24.14.1-slim`
- `@earendil-works/pi-ai` 0.80.6, `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent` 0.80.6

title: Code Review step fails immediately with no-worktree-for-write-node
body:
Bug description: The built-in "Coding" workflow's pre-merge Code Review step fails instantly (~38ms, before any actual review runs) with the error: Code Review failed before producing a verdict: no-worktree-for-write-node

Observed context: Task FN-003 ran the default "Coding" workflow (plan then execute then code review then merge). The executor step(s) completed successfully and reported the task as done. The workflow graph then terminated with failure at the code-review-remediation node, with the Code Review step showing status FAILED and the above error as output. This task was created via API (not through the UI "New Task" button), which may be related, possibly the write step didn't get a git worktree registered the way UI-created tasks do.

Expected behavior: The Code Review step should locate the worktree produced by the preceding write step and produce a review verdict, rather than failing outright.

Impact: Tasks (at least those created via API) can complete their actual work successfully but still end up marked as Failed due to this review-stage error, requiring manual retry.

title: TaskDeletedError race: soft-delete mid-heartbeat moveTask strands agent in error
body:
## Summary

Fusion throws a platform-internal `TaskDeletedError` when a task is soft-deleted concurrently with a heartbeat-driven `moveTask` on that same task. The exception is raised from the engine's own task-board / workflow path (not application code), leaves the affected agent in `error` state with a non-empty `lastError`, and requires an external stop/start recovery cycle.

This is the same broad "state-machine operation races with concurrent deletion/lifecycle change" family as:

- https://github.com/Runfusion/Fusion/issues/1945 (stranded cards after out-of-band/workspace merges)
- https://github.com/Runfusion/Fusion/issues/1946 (code-review node fails with no feedback — dispatch/state-persistence race)

There is **no** `TaskDeletedError` symbol or `moveTask` handler in the observing project's application tree (`backend/app/`). The defect is entirely inside the Fusion engine.

---

## Environment

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Platform | Fusion workflow / task-board engine (heartbeat + soft-delete paths) |
| Affected agent | Software Engineer 1 (`agent-e5064b1f`), role `executor` |
| Trigger path | Heartbeat timer → `moveTask` on an in-progress task |
| Concurrent mutation | Soft-delete of the same task (`deletedAt` set / task removed from live board) |
| Task store | `.fusion/fusion.db` (SQLite) |
| Observing project | NextGenEHS (`Automata-intelligentsia/NextGenEHS`) |
| Observation date | 2026-07-13 |

---

## Confirmed reproduction instance

### Instance 1 — NEXT-1247 soft-deleted mid-`moveTask` (2026-07-13)

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Victim task | `NEXT-1247` — "Fix backend healthcheck timeouts under load..." |
| Affected agent | `agent-e5064b1f` (Software Engineer 1) |
| Soft-delete time | `2026-07-13T10:18:51` (engine soft-delete / archive path) |
| Concurrent operation | Agent heartbeat mid-`moveTask` against `NEXT-1247` |
| Exception | `TaskDeletedError` (engine-internal; not present in app source) |
| Agent outcome | Agent entered `error` state with non-empty `lastError` |
| Recovery | Manual `fn agent stop` → `fn agent start` via recovery task `NEXT-1256`; agent returned to `active` and heartbeat resumed |
| Follow-up tracking | `NEXT-1253` (investigation + upstream filing); `NEXT-1256` (agent recovery) |

### Sequence (externally observed)

1. Agent `agent-e5064b1f` was running a heartbeat that attempted a board `moveTask` involving `NEXT-1247`.
2. Concurrently, `NEXT-1247` was soft-deleted (archived / removed from the live board) at approximately `2026-07-13T10:18:51`.
3. The in-flight `moveTask` hit a deleted-task guard and raised `TaskDeletedError`.
4. The exception was recorded as the agent's `lastError` and left the agent stuck in `error` rather than being treated as a benign "task no longer exists" condition for a concurrent lifecycle change.
5. A separate recovery task (`NEXT-1256`) had to stop/start the agent to restore liveness.

### App-side negative check

A repository-wide search of the observing project's application code (`backend/app/`) found:

- **No** `TaskDeletedError` symbol
- **No** `moveTask` handler

The crash originates in Fusion engine internals only.

---

## Impact

- **Agent hard-fail on a concurrent lifecycle race.** Soft-delete is a normal board operation. A heartbeat that races with it should not put an otherwise healthy agent permanently into `error`.
- **Requires manual recovery.** Observed recovery needed an explicit stop/start (`NEXT-1256`). Until then the agent could not take new work.
- **False "agent is broken" signal.** Operators and health watchers treat non-empty `lastError` + `error` state as agent failure, even though the root cause is a board-state race rather than agent misconfiguration or app code.
- **Same family as prior state-machine races.** Related closed soft-delete work (#750, #813, #987, #1016) addressed other soft-delete gaps; this specific heartbeat-`moveTask` race still surfaces in the field.

---

## Candidate-cause assessment

> Black-box observation only — engine source is not visible from this repository. Hypotheses ranked by consistency with evidence, not confirmed root cause.

| Hypothesis | Consistent? | Notes |
|------------|-------------|-------|
| **Missing soft-delete awareness in heartbeat `moveTask`** | ⚠️ Leading | Soft-delete sets `deletedAt` / removes the live row while `moveTask` still holds a pre-delete task reference. The deleted-task guard throws hard instead of treating the race as a no-op / cancel. |
| **Non-atomic "check then move" on live task row** | ⚠️ Likely | If `moveTask` reads the task, then later writes a column transition without re-checking `deletedAt` under the same transaction/lock, a concurrent soft-delete wins and the write path raises. |
| **Error classification too severe for concurrent deletion** | ⚠️ Likely | Even if detection is correct, classifying a concurrent soft-delete during heartbeat as a fatal agent `lastError` (instead of a transient/benign board miss) is what strands the agent. |
| **Application-code bug** | ❌ Ruled out | No `TaskDeletedError` / `moveTask` symbols in observing app tree; exception is engine-owned. |

---

## Requested action

Please investigate the heartbeat → `moveTask` path under concurrent soft-delete, focusing on:

1. **Why does `moveTask` throw a hard `TaskDeletedError` when the target was soft-deleted mid-flight?** A concurrent soft-delete during heartbeat should be a benign miss (no-op / cancelled move), not a fatal agent error.
2. **Is the live-task read and column write atomic w.r.t. `deletedAt`?** If not, please serialize or re-check deletion under the same transaction/lock used for the move.
3. **Should concurrent-deletion races be excluded from agent `lastError` / `error` state?** Even if the move fails, the agent should remain `active` with an empty (or clearly non-fatal) error so heartbeat liveness is preserved.
4. **Can soft-delete cancel in-flight board mutations for the deleted task?** Related prior work (#750 "Ensure soft deleted tasks are immediately stopped processing", #813 "Block agent writes to soft-deleted tasks") suggests intent to stop processing; this race indicates a remaining window on the heartbeat `moveTask` path.
5. **Please preserve a durable audit line** for this race (task id, agent id, soft-delete timestamp, move attempt timestamp) so field reports can correlate without relying on ephemeral agent `lastError` text.

We do **not** prescribe a specific engine-code fix — internals are not visible from this repository. Evidence above is from the engine's own observable outputs (agent state, messages, recovery tasks, board history).

---

## Related references

| Reference | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| Observing tasks | `NEXT-1253` (investigation + upstream filing), `NEXT-1247` (soft-deleted victim), `NEXT-1256` (SE1 stop/start recovery) |
| Affected agent | `agent-e5064b1f` (Software Engineer 1) |
| https://github.com/Runfusion/Fusion/issues/1945 | Sibling state-machine race family (stranded cards) |
| https://github.com/Runfusion/Fusion/issues/1946 | Sibling dispatch/state-persistence race |
| https://github.com/Runfusion/Fusion/issues/750 | Prior: stop processing soft-deleted tasks |
| https://github.com/Runfusion/Fusion/issues/813 | Prior: block agent writes to soft-deleted tasks |
| https://github.com/Runfusion/Fusion/issues/987 | Prior: blockedBy recovery for soft-deleted blockers |
| https://github.com/Runfusion/Fusion/issues/1016 | Prior: skip soft-deleted tasks in deadlock detection |

---

## Filing metadata

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Reported by | NextGenEHS project — Fusion Platform Liaison (`agent-1ef97225`) |
| Filing channel | `gh issue create --repo Runfusion/Fusion` (account `Automata-intelligentsia`, `repo` scope) |
| Scope note | Black-box, externally-observed bug report. Engine source was not examined. |
| Date | 2026-07-13 |

title: Routing-loop defect: tasks explicitly routed to a workflow revert to triage despite persisted workflow selection
body:
## Summary

Tasks explicitly routed to a workflow (e.g., via `fn_workflow_select` to `builtin:coding`) revert to triage despite the workflow selection being persisted in the board state.

## Observed pattern

- NEXT-718 / NEXT-1139 cycling: triage → builtin:coding → triage
- NEXT-1167 / 1168 / 1178 self-blocking in the same queue
- The EM clarification notes that NEXT-1167 is a diagnosis card and not a routing target, yet the loop continues regardless of assigned workflow.

## Recommended fix scope

The Fusion engine scheduler routing should honor persisted workflow selection when scheduling task execution. A board-side persisted workflow_id/selection is not being respected through at least one scheduler routing pass, causing visible back-and-forth column cycling rather than a stable assignment.

## Linked task IDs for reproduction/context

- NEXT-718
- NEXT-1139
- NEXT-1167
- NEXT-1168
- NEXT-1178

## Environment notes

Reported from a Fusion project run; board-integrity evidence and scheduler timestamps are available in the linked task trace.

title: 0.59.0: all bundled example plugins (dependency-graph, grok-runtime, roadmap, acp-runtime, compound-engineering) fail with "Cannot find package '@fusion/core'"
body:
**Version:** @runfusion/fusion 0.59.0 (npm global install)

**Bug:** Every bundled example plugin shipped in `dist/plugins/*/bundled.js` imports from the internal, unpublished, private workspace package `@fusion/core` via the plugin-sdk re-export, and that import is never inlined by the bundler — it's left as an unresolved external `import`. Since `@fusion/core` is a private monorepo package (not published to npm), it can never resolve outside the Fusion source repo, so every one of these plugins crashes the moment it's enabled:

```
Cannot find package '@fusion/core' imported from /path/to/@runfusion/fusion/dist/plugins/fusion-plugin-dependency-graph/bundled.js
```

Confirmed present (via `grep -c 'from "@fusion/core"'`) in every bundled plugin under `dist/plugins/`:
- fusion-plugin-dependency-graph
- fusion-plugin-grok-runtime
- fusion-plugin-roadmap
- fusion-plugin-acp-runtime
- fusion-plugin-compound-engineering

Each bundle contains the same unresolved chunk:

```js
// ../plugin-sdk/src/index.ts
import {
  WORKFLOW_EXTENSION_SCHEMA_VERSION,
  workflowExtensionRegistryId,
  createBoardActionServices
} from "@fusion/core";
```

**Repro:**
1. `npm install -g @runfusion/fusion@0.59.0`
2. `fn daemon` / `fn dashboard`
3. Enable any bundled example plugin (e.g. Grok Runtime, Dependency Graph) for a project via the dashboard's Command Center \u2192 Plugins tab, or `fn plugin enable <id>` for that project
4. Plugin immediately errors: `Cannot find package '@fusion/core' imported from .../bundled.js`

**Impact:** the new Command Center Plugins tab (added in 0.59.0) surfaces this immediately \u2014 every bundled plugin shows a red ERROR badge on enable, and Grok Runtime (a documented model-provider integration, not just a demo) is completely unusable.

**Likely cause:** the esbuild/rollup config for the `plugins/*` bundling target treats `@fusion/core` as external instead of inlining it (similar area to #1336, but that fix predates this and didn't cover this regression \u2014 confirmed broken as of the current 0.59.0 npm publish).

**Workaround:** none client-side; `@fusion/core` isn't published anywhere resolvable. Only fix is uninstalling/never-enabling the affected plugins (`fn plugin uninstall <id> --force`) until a corrected build is published.

title: Zombie-timer scheduler defect: heartbeat not firing while timer entry remains present (heartbeatTimerRepair staleRepairReason)
body:
## Summary
The Fusion heartbeat scheduler exhibits a **zombie-timer** defect: the agent remains in `active` state with a timer entry present, but no heartbeat fires for an extended period. The heartbeat recovery subsystem detects and repairs the stale state, but the underlying scheduler defect should be investigated upstream.

## Observed agent
- **Agent:** `agent-09dcf8b2` (CEO)
- **State:** active
- **lastError:** (empty)
- **Recovery metadata found by heartbeat watchdog:**
  ```
  metadata.heartbeatTimerRepair.staleRepairReason = 'zombie-timer-rearmed: no heartbeat for 62348s while a timer entry remained present (threshold 600s)'
  ```

## What should happen vs. what is happening
- **Expected:** While the agent is in `active` state and a heartbeat timer entry exists, the scheduler should fire heartbeats at the configured interval so `lastHeartbeatAt` stays fresh.
- **Actual:** The timer entry is present but no heartbeat fires for ~62 348 seconds (≈17 hours). The engine's own heartbeat-repair mechanism eventually re-arms the timer, but this is a stop-gap; the root cause is unaddressed.

## Reproduction context
This was detected by the project's `heartbeat_freshness_watchdog` script (nextgenehs repo, `scripts/heartbeat_freshness_watchdog.py`) as part of routine board monitoring. The agent recovered via the watchdog's cyclic stop/start path; the staleRepairReason field was captured verbatim and is included above.

## Request
1. Investigate why the scheduler timer is not firing despite the agent being in `active` state and a timer entry being present.
2. Determine whether this is a timer re-arm race, a scheduler queue stall, or a state-persistence bug.
3. Ship a durable fix so the heartbeat wake-up mechanism cannot silently drop for an extended window while the agent remains nominally active.

title: execute_loop_stall: execute → pause-abort → re-queue-to-todo cycles without terminal progression
body:
## Bug Report: execute_loop_stall

### Fusion Task Affected
**Task:** NEXT-1184

### Observed Behavior
NEXT-1184 has cycled through 36+ consecutive execute → pause-abort → re-queue-to-todo transitions while remaining stuck in an indefinite in-progress state. The task shows no terminal progression: it never lands in a done/archived/error column and continues cycling instead.

### Signature / Classification
This matches the documented `execute_loop_stall` engine-defect pattern captured in the Fusion board-integrity scanner (`scripts/board_integrity_scanner.py`): an *execution-phase* loop with ≥10 cycles through execute → pause-abort → re-queue-to-todo without ever reaching a terminal state.

**Detector:** `execute_loop_stall` from the board integrity scanner

### Description
During normal workflow execution the engine appears unable to advance from the `execute` step to a terminal step. Instead of completing or failing outright, the step is aborted (`pause-abort`) and the task is re-queued to `todo`, where it is dispatched for execution again — perpetuating the cycle indefinitely with no terminal progression.

This is distinct from a planning-phase loop. It affects the *execution* phase of a builtin/standard coding workflow and has no equivalent bounded-retry or kill mechanism.

### Request
Please investigate the engine dispatch / state-persistence bug that causes tasks to cycle through execute → pause-abort → re-queue-to-todo indefinitely. A durable fix should either:

1. Allow the workflow to advance to a terminal state after a configurable maximum cycle count, or
2. Surface a proper `error` / terminal state so the task can be reviewed and resolved.

### Context
- This signature was observed live on the NextGenEHS project board and is flagged as a genuine stuck-engine condition by the local board-integrity watcher.
- Upstream issue for the related sibling signature (board-card state-machine stranding) is #1945; this appears to be a distinct execution-dispatch bug.
